Question: Why does the ENT doctor ask so many questions before even looking in my ear, nose, or throat?
Answer: The questions help the clinician understand the pattern, duration, severity, and possible causes of your symptoms before examining you. A careful history often narrows the likely diagnosis and helps decide which part of the ear, nose, throat, head, or neck needs focused examination.
Answer Context: ENT symptoms are often non-specific at first presentation, so history-taking is used to convert a broad complaint into a clinically meaningful pattern. The presenting complaint, onset, progression, previous episodes, treatment response, medical history, medication use, allergy history, family background, and quality-of-life impact all contribute to differential diagnosis and examination planning.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > General ENT History > Principles and structure of history taking

Question: Why did the doctor ask when my symptoms started and whether I had this problem before?
Answer: Timing and recurrence are important because they show whether the problem is sudden, ongoing, recurrent, or worsening. This can help distinguish infections, inflammatory problems, structural issues, or chronic ENT conditions.
Answer Context: The history of the presenting complaint clarifies onset, duration, severity, progression, and recurrence. Previous episodes and response to earlier treatment provide diagnostic direction because recurrent symptoms may suggest chronic disease, unresolved inflammation, anatomical predisposition, or an episodic condition rather than a single isolated event.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > General ENT History > History of presenting complaint and previous episodes

Question: Why was I asked about medicines, allergies, vaccines, and even bleeding or bruising when I came in for an ENT issue?
Answer: These details help assess safety and possible causes. Medicines, allergies, vaccination history, and bleeding tendency can affect diagnosis, examination, treatment options, and whether a procedure may carry extra risk.
Answer Context: Drug history includes prescribed and over-the-counter medication because ENT symptoms may be influenced by current treatment, and future treatment must avoid unsafe drug interactions or allergies. A systems review that includes bleeding or bruising tendency is clinically relevant before procedures, nasal bleeding assessment, or decisions involving cautery, packing, or surgery.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > General ENT History > Drug history, allergies, systems review

Question: Why did the doctor look around my ear before putting the scope inside?
Answer: The outside of the ear can show clues such as swelling, redness, scars, asymmetry, abnormal shape, infection, trauma, or previous surgery. Looking externally first helps guide the internal examination.
Answer Context: Ear examination begins with inspection because the pinna, pre-auricular region, and post-auricular region may reveal clinically relevant findings before otoscopy. Differences in pinna size, protrusion, scars, swelling, erythema, sinuses, fistulae, tenderness, or post-auricular swelling can indicate congenital abnormalities, infection, inflammation, trauma, mastoiditis, lymphadenitis, or previous operative history.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Inspection
Figure Captions: Figure 2: Congenital Microtia of the External Auditory Canal

Question: Why does the doctor pull my ear upwards and backwards before looking inside?
Answer: In adults, this helps straighten the ear canal so the eardrum can be seen more clearly. In children, the direction may be different because their ear canal anatomy is not the same as in adults.
Answer Context: The external auditory canal is not a straight tube, so manipulation of the pinna improves alignment for otoscopy. In adults, pulling the pinna upwards and backwards helps straighten the canal and improves visualisation of the tympanic membrane. In children, pulling downwards and backwards may provide a better view because of anatomical differences.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Otoscopy
Figure Captions: Figure 3: Normal right tympanic membrane

Question: What is the doctor looking for when they shine the otoscope into my ear?
Answer: The clinician checks the ear canal for wax, discharge, redness, swelling, or injury, and then checks the eardrum for colour, position, light reflex, perforation, bulging, or retraction.
Answer Context: Otoscopy systematically evaluates the external auditory canal and tympanic membrane. Canal wax, discharge, erythema, or swelling may indicate obstruction, infection, or trauma. Tympanic membrane colour, translucency, light reflex, position, and integrity help identify inflammation, middle-ear infection, retraction, perforation, tympanosclerosis, or other middle-ear pathology.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Otoscopy
Figure Captions: Figure 3: Normal right tympanic membrane

Question: What does it mean if my eardrum looks red, white, bulging, or pulled in?
Answer: These appearances can suggest different problems. Redness may occur with inflammation or infection, white plaques may suggest scarring, bulging may occur with infection, and a pulled-in eardrum can occur with pressure or middle-ear disease.
Answer Context: The tympanic membrane is normally greyish and translucent with a light reflex. Red or pink colouring may suggest infection or inflammation, while white plaques can indicate tympanosclerosis. Bulging suggests pressure from acute middle-ear infection, whereas retraction may occur with Eustachian tube dysfunction, chronic middle-ear disease, or cholesteatoma risk.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Tympanic membrane findings
Figure Captions: Figure 3: Normal right tympanic membrane

Question: Why did the doctor whisper numbers and words to test my hearing?
Answer: Whisper and speech testing provide a quick screening estimate of hearing ability. It is not as precise as a formal audiogram, but it helps identify whether further hearing tests may be needed.
Answer Context: Free-field speech testing is a bedside screening method for hearing loss. The clinician uses whispered, conversational, and loud voice at different distances while masking the opposite ear to reduce cross-hearing. The patient’s ability to repeat words, numbers, or child-friendly phrases provides a rough estimate of functional hearing threshold.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Free field speech testing

Question: Why was a tuning fork placed on my forehead and near my ear?
Answer: Tuning fork tests help distinguish whether hearing loss is more likely due to a problem conducting sound through the outer or middle ear, or due to inner ear or nerve-related hearing loss.
Answer Context: Weber’s and Rinne’s tests compare bone conduction and air conduction. Weber’s test assesses lateralisation of sound through bone conduction, while Rinne’s test compares hearing through the mastoid bone and air near the ear canal. Together, they help differentiate conductive hearing loss from sensorineural hearing loss.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Weber’s and Rinne’s tests
Figure Captions: Figure 4: Weber’s test || Figure 5: Rinne’s test

Question: What does it mean if I hear the tuning fork better behind my ear than in front of it?
Answer: That result can suggest conductive hearing loss in that ear, meaning sound is not travelling normally through the outer or middle ear. A clinician usually interprets this together with other findings.
Answer Context: In a normal ear, air conduction is louder than bone conduction, called a positive Rinne test. If bone conduction is louder than air conduction, this is a negative Rinne test and suggests external or middle-ear disease affecting sound conduction. Interpretation is strengthened by combining it with Weber’s test and otoscopy.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Ear Examination > Rinne’s test interpretation
Figure Captions: Figure 4: Weber’s test || Figure 5: Rinne’s test

Question: Why did the doctor look at my nose from the front, side, and underneath?
Answer: Different viewing angles show different features of the nose, including deviation, asymmetry, scars, collapse, bumps, tip position, and signs of previous surgery.
Answer Context: External nasal inspection assesses form and structural alignment. The front view reveals shape, midline deviation, asymmetry, scars, and skin changes. The side view shows dorsal profile, humps, collapse, projection, and tip rotation. The basal view helps assess nostril symmetry, deviation, and surgical scars.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Nasal Examination > Inspection
Figure Captions: Figure 6: Separation of the nose into bony pyramid, cartilaginous pyramid and lobule. The upper third mostly composed of bony vault, middle third made up of dorsal septum and upper lateral cartilages, and lower third or nasal tip composed primarily of lower lateral cartilages, septum and soft tissue). || Figure 7: Upper, middle and lower thirds of the nose.

Question: What is the metal instrument used to open my nostril during examination?
Answer: It is commonly a nasal speculum. It gently opens the nostril so the clinician can inspect the front part of the nasal cavity, including the septum, turbinates, blood vessels, and possible polyps.
Answer Context: Anterior rhinoscopy uses a nasal speculum and light source to examine the anterior nasal cavity. Proper handling keeps the clinician’s hand from blocking the view and allows inspection of the septum, inferior turbinates, mucosa, secretions, vessels, perforations, and visible nasal masses or polyps.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Nasal Examination > Anterior rhinoscopy
Figure Captions: Figure 8: How to perform anterior rhinoscopy with a Thudicum’s speculum.

Question: Why was the doctor looking for swelling, clear fluid, blood vessels, or polyps inside my nose?
Answer: These findings can point to common causes of blocked nose, runny nose, nosebleeds, or sinus-related symptoms. They help distinguish structural problems from inflammatory or allergic causes.
Answer Context: Common anterior rhinoscopy findings include septal deviation, inferior turbinate swelling, oedema, clear rhinorrhoea, septal perforation, prominent blood vessels over Little’s area, and inflammatory nasal polyps. These findings help relate symptoms to obstruction, rhinitis, epistaxis tendency, mucosal inflammation, or sinonasal pathology.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Nasal Examination > Common pathological findings
Figure Captions: Figure 9: Septal deviation to the right || Figure 10: Right Inferior Turbinate swelling due to allergic rhinitis || Figure 11: Septal perforation when viewed through the right nasal airway || Figure 12: Prominent blood vessels overlying right Little’s area (anterior septum) || Figure 13: Left-sided inflammatory nasal polyp

Question: Why did the doctor hold a cold metal piece under my nose and ask me to breathe out?
Answer: This is a simple way to check whether air is passing through both nostrils. Moist breath creates mist on the cold metal surface, which gives a quick impression of nasal airflow.
Answer Context: Nasal misting uses a cold metal tongue depressor to assess bilateral nasal patency. Humidified expired air condenses on the cold surface, producing visible mist. Because it is quick, simple, and non-invasive, it is particularly useful in children or patients who may not tolerate more invasive nasal examination.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Nasal Examination > Nasal misting
Figure Captions: Figure 14: Cold spatula misting test. The humidified expired air creates a mist on the cold metal tongue depressor. Due to its simplicity and lack of invasiveness, it is especially useful to assess bilateral nasal patency in paediatric patients.

Question: Why did the doctor lift the side of my nose or use a small probe while I breathed in?
Answer: This checks whether narrowing or collapse of the nasal valve is contributing to your blocked nose. If breathing improves when the area is supported, it suggests that part of the obstruction may be structural.
Answer Context: Cottle’s and modified Cottle’s tests assess nasal valve patency and alar collapse. The internal nasal valve is the narrowest part of the nasal airway and may be narrowed by septal deviation, enlarged inferior turbinates, or collapsed nasal cartilages. Mechanical support during inspiration helps identify valve-related obstruction.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Nasal Examination > Cottle’s and modified Cottle’s test
Figure Captions: Figure 15: Modified Cottle’s test

Question: Why might an ENT doctor pass a flexible camera through my nose?
Answer: Flexible nasendoscopy allows a more complete view of the nasal cavity and areas beyond it, including sinus drainage pathways, the post-nasal space, throat, voice box, and lower throat.
Answer Context: Flexible nasendoscopy provides direct endoscopic assessment beyond what anterior rhinoscopy can show. It permits inspection of the nasal cavity, sinus drainage pathways, post-nasal space, Eustachian tube orifice, fossa of Rosenmüller, oropharynx, larynx, and hypopharynx, helping detect pathology hidden from simple anterior examination.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Nasal Examination > Flexible nasendoscopy
Figure Captions: Figure 16: Endoscopic view of the right anterior nasal cavity. || Figure 17: Endoscopic view of the right middle metal region. || Figure 18: Endoscopic view of the right post-nasal space. The Eustachian tube orifice is where the middle ear drains down into the nasal cavity. The Fossa of Rosenmueller is an important place to check for pathology such as nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

Question: Why did the doctor ask me to swallow while feeling the lump in my neck?
Answer: A lump that moves upward when you swallow may be related to the thyroid gland. This helps the clinician decide whether the swelling is likely thyroid-related or from another neck structure.
Answer Context: Neck palpation uses movement with swallowing to assess anatomical origin. Thyroid tissue usually elevates during swallowing because of its relationship with the larynx and pretracheal fascia. Observing or feeling upward movement helps distinguish thyroid swellings from other neck masses such as lymph nodes, cysts, or soft tissue lesions.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Neck and thyroid status exam > Palpation
Figure Captions: Figure 19: Correct hand position for thyroid examination

Question: Why did the doctor ask me to stick my tongue out while checking a lump under my chin or in the middle of my neck?
Answer: A midline lump that moves when the tongue is protruded may suggest a thyroglossal cyst. This movement gives a clue about where the lump comes from.
Answer Context: Midline neck masses are assessed dynamically. A thyroglossal cyst may move upward when the tongue is protruded because it is related to the embryological tract between the tongue base and thyroid region. This sign helps separate thyroglossal lesions from other midline or thyroid-related swellings.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Neck and thyroid status exam > Tongue protrusion test
Figure Captions: Figure 19: Correct hand position for thyroid examination

Question: Why did the doctor feel different areas under my jaw, behind my ears, and above my collarbone?
Answer: The clinician was checking the lymph nodes of the neck. Enlarged lymph nodes can occur with infection, inflammation, or more serious causes, so their location and features are important.
Answer Context: Systematic lymph node examination includes submental, submandibular, pre-auricular, post-auricular, occipital, anterior cervical, posterior cervical, and supraclavicular regions. Distribution, tenderness, consistency, size, fixation, and associated neck findings help differentiate inflammatory, infective, and neoplastic patterns.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Neck and thyroid status exam > Lymph node examination

Question: Why did the doctor listen to my thyroid with a stethoscope?
Answer: Listening over the thyroid can detect a bruit, which is a sound caused by increased blood flow. This may be seen in some overactive thyroid conditions.
Answer Context: Thyroid auscultation is used to detect a bruit, which reflects increased vascular flow through the gland. In the appropriate clinical context, this may support hyperthyroidism. It is interpreted alongside pulse rate, hand signs, sweating, tremor, eye signs, and other thyroid status findings.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Neck and thyroid status exam > Auscultation

Question: Why did the doctor check my hands, pulse, eyes, and ankle reflexes during a thyroid examination?
Answer: Thyroid disease can affect the whole body, not just the neck. The doctor checks for signs of overactive or underactive thyroid function, such as tremor, sweating, pulse changes, eye changes, or altered reflexes.
Answer Context: Thyroid status examination assesses systemic signs. Hyperthyroidism may produce tremor, sweating, warm hands, palmar erythema, tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, thyroid acropachy, lid lag, Graves’ eye disease, and brisk reflexes. Hypothyroidism may produce bradycardia, characteristic facial complexion, eyebrow thinning, pretibial myxoedema, and slowed reflex responses.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Neck and thyroid status exam > Thyroid status examination
Figure Captions: Figure 20: Thyroid acropachy. || Figure 21: Proptosis secondary to thyroid eye disease.

Question: Why was I asked to raise my arms above my head during a thyroid check?
Answer: This checks whether a large thyroid swelling is pressing into the upper chest area. If raising the arms causes facial congestion, neck vein swelling, or voice change, it may suggest pressure from a retrosternal goitre.
Answer Context: Pemberton’s test assesses possible thoracic outlet or superior vena cava obstruction from a retrosternal goitre. Raising the arms may provoke facial or neck venous congestion and hoarseness if the enlarged thyroid compresses structures at the thoracic inlet. It is used as an adjunct to inspection, palpation, percussion, and thyroid status assessment.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Neck and thyroid status exam > Pemberton’s test

Question: Why did the doctor check my eye movements during an ENT appointment?
Answer: Eye movements can be affected by skull base problems, orbital infection, or complications of ear disease. This check helps identify whether symptoms involve nerves beyond the ear, nose, or throat alone.
Answer Context: Examination of optic and ocular motor nerves assesses colour vision and eye movements. These functions may be affected by skull base tumours or orbital infections. In otology, Gradenigo’s syndrome is a recognised complication of acute otitis media involving unilateral periorbital pain, diplopia from sixth nerve palsy, and petrositis.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Cranial Nerves Examination > CN II, III, IV, VI

Question: Why did the doctor touch different parts of my face with cotton wool?
Answer: This checks the sensory branches of the trigeminal nerve, which supplies feeling to the forehead, cheek, and jaw areas. Unequal sensation can help localise nerve involvement.
Answer Context: The trigeminal nerve has sensory divisions over the ophthalmic, maxillary, and mandibular territories. Light touch testing compares left and right forehead, cheek, and mandibular regions. This is paired with motor assessment of masseter, temporalis, and pterygoid function to identify sensory or motor trigeminal impairment.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Cranial Nerves Examination > CN V

Question: Why did the clinician ask me to raise my eyebrows, close my eyes tightly, puff my cheeks, and smile?
Answer: These movements test the facial nerve, which controls the muscles of facial expression. Weakness patterns can help determine the type and possible location of facial nerve involvement.
Answer Context: Facial nerve examination assesses resting tone and motor function across branches emerging through the parotid gland. Raising the eyebrows, closing the eyes tightly, puffing the cheeks, smiling, and grimacing test facial expression muscles. Forehead involvement helps distinguish lower motor neuron facial palsy from forehead-sparing patterns.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Cranial Nerves Examination > CN VII

Question: Why did the doctor ask me to say “ah” and listen to my voice and cough?
Answer: This checks nerves involved in the throat, palate, voice, and swallowing. Voice change, weak cough, or abnormal palate movement can suggest vagus or related nerve problems.
Answer Context: Glossopharyngeal and vagus nerve assessment includes palate movement during phonation, uvular deviation, voice quality, cough strength, swallowing, and gag reflex. A bovine-sounding cough or hoarseness may suggest recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement, while uvular deviation helps localise palatal weakness.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Cranial Nerves Examination > CN IX and CN X

Question: Why did the doctor ask me to shrug my shoulders, turn my head, and stick out my tongue?
Answer: These movements test the accessory and hypoglossal nerves. They help assess neck muscle strength and tongue movement, which can be affected by nerve problems in the head and neck region.
Answer Context: The accessory nerve is examined by shoulder shrug against resistance for trapezius function and head turn for sternocleidomastoid function. The hypoglossal nerve is assessed by inspecting tongue bulk, moving the tongue side to side, and protrusion; deviation of the tongue tip occurs toward the side of the lesion.
Handbook Reference: Generic Skills in ENT > Cranial Nerves Examination > CN XI and CN XII

Question: Why does the doctor say the ear has three parts when I only see the outside part?
Answer: The visible ear is only the external part. The ear also has a middle ear behind the eardrum and an inner ear deeper in the temporal bone, which are important for hearing and balance.
Answer Context: The ear is divided into external, middle, and inner components. The external ear collects sound and leads it through the ear canal to the tympanic membrane. The middle ear contains an air-filled cavity and ossicles that transmit and amplify sound. The inner ear contains the cochlea for hearing transduction and vestibular organs for balance detection.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Ear Anatomy > External, middle, and inner ear; revised PDF pp. 43–49
Figure Captions: Figure 22: External auditory canal and middle ear (Elf-ENT)

Question: Why can an injury to the outer ear turn into a cauliflower-looking ear?
Answer: The ear cartilage depends on its covering layer for nutrition. If blood, infection, or inflammation separates that layer from the cartilage after trauma, the cartilage can be damaged and heal with deformity.
Answer Context: The pinna is composed mainly of elastic cartilage covered by perichondrium. When trauma separates cartilage from perichondrium, cartilage nutrition is compromised. Blood collection, infection, or inflammation can lead to cartilage necrosis and later fibrotic deformity, producing the characteristic cauliflower ear appearance.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Ear Anatomy > Cauliflower ear; revised PDF p. 45
Figure Captions: Figure 24: Cauliflower ear (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why can ear problems affect both hearing and balance?
Answer: The inner ear contains structures for both hearing and balance. The cochlea handles sound, while the semicircular canals, utricle, and saccule detect head movement and position.
Answer Context: The cochlea converts sound energy into electrical impulses interpreted by the brain. The semicircular canals detect angular head acceleration, while the utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration. Because these sensory structures are anatomically close within the inner ear, inner-ear disease may produce hearing symptoms, balance symptoms, or both.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Ear Anatomy > Inner ear functions; revised PDF p. 49

Question: I feel like the room is spinning. Is that different from just feeling dizzy?
Answer: Yes. A spinning sensation is called true vertigo and is different from general light-headedness or imbalance. The distinction helps determine whether the problem is likely related to the inner ear, brain, or another system.
Answer Context: True vertigo is classically experienced as spinning or movement of the environment. It must be distinguished from disequilibrium, faintness, or non-specific dizziness because the pattern of symptoms directs the diagnostic pathway. Duration, frequency, onset, and associated hearing symptoms help differentiate peripheral vestibular disease from central causes.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Dizziness and Vertigo > Description and differential approach; revised PDF p. 50

Question: Why does the ENT doctor ask how long my spinning attacks last?
Answer: The duration of vertigo attacks is one of the most useful clues. Brief position-triggered attacks, prolonged continuous vertigo, and repeated attacks with hearing symptoms suggest different causes.
Answer Context: Vertigo disorders are separated clinically by onset, frequency, duration, and associated audiovestibular symptoms. BPPV is usually positional and brief, vestibular neuritis may cause continuous vertigo for more than 24 hours, Meniere’s disease is associated with fluctuating hearing loss and aural fullness, and vestibular migraine may last minutes to days.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Dizziness and Vertigo > Causes and symptoms; revised PDF pp. 50–51

Question: Why do I get dizzy when I turn over in bed or move my head quickly?
Answer: That pattern can fit benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, or BPPV. It is commonly triggered by head movement and is often assessed using the Dix-Hallpike test.
Answer Context: BPPV is a common cause of true vertigo and typically causes rotatory vertigo with head movement. A positive Dix-Hallpike test supports the diagnosis. The mechanism involves displaced otoconia within the vestibular system, and repositioning manoeuvres aim to move these crystals away from the sensitive canal region.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Dizziness and Vertigo > BPPV; revised PDF pp. 50–52

Question: Why does my dizziness come with ear fullness, ringing, and changing hearing?
Answer: Vertigo with fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and a feeling of fullness in the ear can suggest Meniere’s disease. The clinician will consider this pattern alongside examination and hearing tests.
Answer Context: Meniere’s disease produces rotatory vertigo associated with fluctuating hearing loss, often affecting low-frequency thresholds. Tinnitus may worsen during attacks, and aural fullness can precede vertigo. The underlying mechanism is thought to involve endolymphatic hydrops, and treatment is adjusted according to severity and response.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Dizziness and Vertigo > Meniere’s disease; revised PDF pp. 51–52

Question: Why might migraine be considered when I came in for vertigo but do not always have a headache?
Answer: Vestibular migraine can cause vertigo even when headache or visual symptoms are not obvious. The clinician may ask about light sensitivity, sound sensitivity, sleep, food triggers, and stress patterns.
Answer Context: Vestibular migraine can present with rotatory vertigo lasting minutes, hours, or days. Headache, photophobia, visual disturbance, and phonophobia may occur but are not always present. Because symptoms can overlap with other vestibular disorders, trigger history and symptom diaries are useful in identifying migraine-related vestibular episodes.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Dizziness and Vertigo > Vestibular migraine; revised PDF pp. 51–52

Question: Why does the doctor want to know if my hearing loss came on suddenly or slowly?
Answer: The timing helps determine urgency and likely cause. Sudden hearing loss may need prompt investigation, while gradual hearing loss may suggest age-related, noise-related, conductive, or chronic middle-ear causes.
Answer Context: Hearing loss is assessed by onset, laterality, and associated otological or neuro-otological symptoms. Sudden sensorineural loss may be associated with tinnitus or vertigo and warrants investigation, whereas gradual bilateral loss may suggest presbyacusis or noise-induced damage. Conductive causes include wax, effusion, perforation, otosclerosis, and cholesteatoma.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Hearing Loss > History and causes; revised PDF pp. 53–54

Question: What is the difference between conductive and nerve-related hearing loss?
Answer: Conductive hearing loss means sound is not travelling efficiently through the outer or middle ear. Sensorineural hearing loss means the inner ear or hearing nerve pathway is affected.
Answer Context: Conductive hearing loss arises when sound transmission through the external canal, tympanic membrane, or middle ear is impaired. Sensorineural hearing loss arises from cochlear or neural dysfunction. Pure tone audiometry and tuning fork tests are complementary because they help distinguish air-bone gaps from inner-ear or nerve-related threshold changes.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Hearing Loss > Conductive and sensorineural hearing loss; revised PDF pp. 53–54

Question: Can earwax really cause hearing loss?
Answer: Yes, but usually only if it blocks the ear canal enough to prevent sound from passing through properly. The clinician can confirm this by examining the ear.
Answer Context: Excessive cerumen can produce conductive hearing loss when it occludes the external auditory canal. Patients may describe a blocked sensation. Otoscopy identifies canal obstruction, and management may include softening drops, microsuction, a Jobson Horne probe, or syringing in appropriate primary-care settings.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Hearing Loss > Excessive earwax and management; revised PDF pp. 53–55

Question: Why might an MRI be suggested for hearing loss in only one ear?
Answer: One-sided or asymmetrical sensorineural hearing loss may need MRI to exclude a vestibular schwannoma, also called an acoustic neuroma.
Answer Context: Asymmetrical sensorineural hearing loss with normal otoscopy can indicate retrocochlear pathology. MRI is used to assess for vestibular schwannoma and other cerebellopontine angle lesions. This is particularly relevant when hearing loss is unilateral, unexplained, or associated with tinnitus or vestibular symptoms.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Hearing Loss > Sensorineural hearing loss; revised PDF p. 54

Question: Why was I told a hearing operation may not guarantee better hearing even if it repairs the eardrum?
Answer: Some procedures mainly aim to stop recurrent infection or make the ear safer and drier. Hearing may improve after repair, but this is not always guaranteed.
Answer Context: Tympanoplasty uses cartilage or temporalis fascia to repair tympanic membrane perforation. The operation may improve hearing by restoring the drum’s integrity, but its usual indications include preventing recurrent ear infection and waterproofing the ear. The final hearing result depends on middle-ear condition, ossicular function, and coexisting disease.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Hearing Loss > Surgical management; revised PDF p. 55

Question: Why do I hear ringing even when there is no sound around me?
Answer: This is called tinnitus. It means you perceive sound without an external sound source, and it is commonly associated with hearing loss or other ear-related causes.
Answer Context: Tinnitus is the perception of sound in the absence of an external stimulus. It may be subjective, heard only by the patient, or objective, detectable by an examiner. Environmental sound often masks tinnitus, so symptoms may become more noticeable in quiet surroundings, particularly at night.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Tinnitus > Description; revised PDF p. 56

Question: Why does my ringing seem worse at night when I am trying to sleep?
Answer: Tinnitus is often more noticeable in quiet settings because there is less background sound to mask it. Worrying about the noise can also make it feel more intrusive.
Answer Context: Tinnitus commonly becomes prominent during silence because ordinary environmental sound no longer masks internal auditory perception. Anxiety and focused attention can heighten awareness of the symptom. Reassurance, coping strategies, tinnitus retraining therapy, noise generators, and hearing aids may reduce its impact in selected patients.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Tinnitus > Treatment; revised PDF p. 58

Question: Why is a heartbeat-like noise in my ear taken more seriously than ordinary ringing?
Answer: Pulsatile tinnitus may come from turbulent blood flow or vascular causes, so it often needs more targeted assessment than non-pulsatile tinnitus.
Answer Context: Pulsatile tinnitus is synchronous with the heartbeat and is usually related to turbulent blood flow reaching the cochlea. Causes include carotid atherosclerosis, vascular malformations, arteriovenous fistulas, and vascular tumours such as glomus tympanicum or glomus jugulare. Imaging may include MR or CT angiography.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Tinnitus > Pulsatile tinnitus; revised PDF pp. 56–57

Question: Why might a hearing aid help my tinnitus if my main complaint is ringing?
Answer: If tinnitus is associated with hearing loss, a hearing aid may improve external sound input and help mask the tinnitus.
Answer Context: Hearing loss can reduce external auditory stimulation, allowing tinnitus to become more prominent. Amplification through a hearing aid may improve hearing and provide a masking effect. This approach is especially relevant when tinnitus coexists with measurable hearing loss on audiological testing.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Tinnitus > Treatment; revised PDF p. 58

Question: Why has one side of my face suddenly become weak or droopy?
Answer: Facial weakness can occur when the facial nerve is affected. Bell’s palsy is common, but it is a diagnosis of exclusion, so other causes such as infection, trauma, stroke, tumour, or ear disease must be considered.
Answer Context: Facial nerve palsy is temporary or permanent paralysis of cranial nerve VII. Idiopathic Bell’s palsy is the most common cause but should only be concluded after considering trauma, Ramsay Hunt syndrome, acute or chronic otitis media, malignant otitis externa, parotid or temporal bone tumour, systemic inflammatory disease, and cerebrovascular events.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Facial Nerve Palsy > Causes and differential diagnoses; revised PDF p. 59

Question: Why did the doctor ask me to raise my eyebrows and close my eyes tightly?
Answer: These movements test different branches of the facial nerve. They help estimate the severity and pattern of the weakness.
Answer Context: Facial nerve function is assessed by observing facial tone and testing branch-specific movements, including eyebrow elevation, tight eye closure, nose wrinkling, cheek puffing, and showing the teeth. Forehead sparing suggests an upper motor neuron pattern, while forehead involvement is more consistent with lower motor neuron facial palsy.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Facial Nerve Palsy > Signs and House-Brackmann grading; revised PDF p. 60

Question: Why is eye care so important when my facial nerve is weak?
Answer: If the eyelid does not close properly, the eye can dry out and the cornea can become damaged. Artificial tears, taping the eyelid, and ophthalmology review may be needed.
Answer Context: Facial nerve palsy can impair eyelid closure, exposing the cornea to dryness and injury. Without eye protection, corneal scarring and even blindness may occur. General management therefore prioritises artificial tears, eyelid protection, and referral to ophthalmology when eye closure is impaired.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Facial Nerve Palsy > Complications and treatment; revised PDF pp. 60–61

Question: Why did the doctor examine my ear when my main problem is facial weakness?
Answer: Some ear conditions can affect the facial nerve, including acute otitis media, chronic ear disease, cholesteatoma, or malignant otitis externa.
Answer Context: The facial nerve travels in close anatomical relation to the middle ear and temporal bone. Otoscopy may reveal acute otitis media or cholesteatoma as causes of facial weakness. Audiometry can identify conductive loss from cholesteatoma or asymmetrical sensorineural loss suggesting a cerebellopontine angle lesion.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Facial Nerve Palsy > Otoscopy and investigations; revised PDF pp. 60–61

Question: Why can my ear hurt even though the doctor says the ear itself looks normal?
Answer: Ear pain can be referred from other areas of the head and neck. Dental problems, jaw-joint problems, throat disease, sinus inflammation, and neck problems can all be felt as ear pain.
Answer Context: Otalgia may originate from the ear or be referred through shared sensory nerve pathways. Cranial nerves V, VII, IX, and X, and cervical nerves C2 and C3, can transmit pain perceived in the ear. A normal eardrum with persistent or severe pain should prompt assessment of dental, nasal, throat, jaw, salivary, laryngeal, and cervical causes.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otalgia > Referred otalgia; revised PDF pp. 62–63

Question: Why does my ear pain get worse when I chew or open my mouth?
Answer: Pain in front of the tragus that worsens with eating can suggest temporomandibular joint dysfunction rather than a primary ear infection.
Answer Context: Temporomandibular joint dysfunction can refer pain to the ear through trigeminal nerve pathways. The eardrum may appear normal, while tenderness over the jaw joint, clicking, misalignment, or pain with chewing supports a jaw-related source rather than otitis media or otitis externa.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otalgia > TMJ dysfunction; revised PDF p. 62

Question: Why is severe ear pain in an older diabetic person concerning?
Answer: Severe ear pain in someone with diabetes or reduced immunity can suggest necrotising otitis externa, a serious infection that can spread to the bone at the skull base.
Answer Context: Necrotising otitis externa is a severe progressive infection of the external ear canal that can extend to the lateral skull base and cause osteomyelitis. It is especially concerning in elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised patients, particularly when pain is severe, unilateral, disproportionate, or associated with granulation tissue or cranial nerve palsies.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otalgia > Necrotising otitis externa red flags; revised PDF p. 62

Question: Why did the doctor ask about throat symptoms when I came in for ear pain?
Answer: Throat disease can refer pain to the ear, so the clinician checks for symptoms that might point to tonsillitis, peritonsillar abscess, or more serious throat pathology.
Answer Context: The glossopharyngeal nerve and vagus nerve provide sensory pathways through which oropharyngeal and laryngeal disease may be perceived as ear pain. Persistent otalgia with a normal ear examination requires careful assessment for referred causes, including peritonsillar abscess, tonsillitis, oropharyngeal carcinoma, and laryngeal cancer.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otalgia > Referred otalgia and red flags; revised PDF p. 63

Question: Is ear discharge always abnormal?
Answer: Not always. Wax discharge can be normal, but pus, blood, mucus, persistent watery discharge, or foul-smelling discharge should be assessed.
Answer Context: Otorrhoea may consist of wax, pus, blood, mucus, or cerebrospinal fluid. Wax can be physiological, whereas purulent, bloody, foul-smelling, chronic, or clear watery discharge may indicate infection, perforation, cholesteatoma, trauma, skull base pathology, or cerebrospinal fluid leak.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otorrhoea > Description; revised PDF p. 64

Question: Why does the doctor ask how long my ear has been discharging?
Answer: Duration helps separate short-term infection from chronic middle-ear disease. A chronic one-sided discharge can raise concern for chronic otitis media or cholesteatoma.
Answer Context: Focused history for otorrhoea includes duration, pain, fever, systemic symptoms, hearing loss, dizziness, foreign body insertion, facial weakness, trauma, and topical antibiotic use. Chronic unilateral discharge, especially with hearing loss or foul smell, suggests chronic otitis media or cholesteatoma.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otorrhoea > History; revised PDF p. 64

Question: Why is clear watery fluid from the ear after head injury worrying?
Answer: Clear watery ear discharge after trauma may represent cerebrospinal fluid leakage and should be assessed urgently.
Answer Context: A history of trauma or skull base surgery with clear watery otorrhoea raises concern for CSF otorrhoea. This differs from wax, pus, mucus, or blood-stained discharge and may indicate a communication between the subarachnoid space and the ear, requiring careful specialist evaluation.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otorrhoea > Differential diagnosis; revised PDF p. 65

Question: Why does my ear hurt more when I pull on it or press the little flap in front of it?
Answer: Pain when moving the pinna or pressing the tragus is typical of inflammation in the external ear canal, called otitis externa.
Answer Context: Otitis externa is inflammation of the external auditory canal. It commonly causes severe otalgia, itching, discharge, and hearing loss. Pain on pinna or tragus movement localises inflammation to the external canal rather than the middle ear, especially when canal swelling, erythema, and purulent discharge are present.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Externa > Symptoms and signs; revised PDF pp. 66–67
Figure Captions: Figure 29: Otitis Externa (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Can swimming or cleaning my ears too much cause an ear canal infection?
Answer: Yes. Swimming, humid conditions, scratching, excessive cleaning, eczema, hearing aids, diabetes, and reduced immunity can all increase the risk of otitis externa.
Answer Context: Otitis externa risk increases when the external canal environment is disrupted. Moisture, trauma from scratching or cleaning, skin disease, impaired ventilation from hearing aids, and immunosuppression can compromise the canal’s protective barrier, allowing bacterial or fungal infection to develop.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Externa > Risk factors; revised PDF p. 66
Figure Captions: Figure 29: Otitis Externa (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why would the doctor put a small sponge-like wick into my ear canal?
Answer: If the canal is very swollen, a Pope wick helps keep it open and allows antibiotic drops to reach the inflamed canal wall.
Answer Context: Severe otitis externa can oedematously narrow or obstruct the external auditory canal, preventing topical drops from reaching the diseased skin. A Pope wick expands when wet, stents the canal open, and improves contact between antibiotic-steroid drops and the canal wall.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Externa > Treatment; revised PDF p. 68
Figure Captions: Figure 29: Otitis Externa (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why might a CT scan be needed for what sounds like an outer ear infection?
Answer: If necrotising otitis externa is suspected, urgent CT imaging is needed because the infection can spread to the skull base bone.
Answer Context: Necrotising otitis externa is a potentially fatal progression of external canal infection into osteomyelitis of the lateral skull base. Severe unilateral pain, diabetes, immunocompromise, poor response to antibiotics, granulation tissue, or cranial nerve involvement justify urgent imaging and hospital-level management.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Externa > Investigations and complications; revised PDF p. 67
Figure Captions: Figure 29: Otitis Externa (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why did my child get an ear infection after a cold?
Answer: A cold can inflame the upper airway and block the Eustachian tube. This can allow infection and fluid to build up in the middle ear.
Answer Context: Acute otitis media often follows an upper respiratory tract infection. Swelling obstructs the Eustachian tube, impairing ventilation and drainage of the middle ear. Ascending infection then causes hyperaemia of the middle-ear mucosa and production of purulent exudate, leading to pain, fever, hearing loss, and a bulging tympanic membrane.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Acute Otitis Media > Causes; revised PDF p. 69
Figure Captions: Figure 30: Bulging tympanic membrane in AOM (Courtesy of Elef ENT).

Question: Why did my child’s ear pain suddenly improve and then start leaking pus?
Answer: If pressure builds behind the eardrum, the eardrum may perforate. Pain can improve when pressure is released, but purulent discharge may then appear.
Answer Context: In acute otitis media, inflammatory exudate accumulates in the middle ear and increases pressure against the tympanic membrane. If the membrane perforates, pressure and pain may reduce abruptly while infected discharge drains through the ear canal, sometimes causing secondary otitis externa.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Acute Otitis Media > Symptoms and signs; revised PDF p. 70
Figure Captions: Figure 30: Bulging tympanic membrane in AOM (Courtesy of Elef ENT).

Question: Does every middle-ear infection need antibiotics immediately?
Answer: Not always. Pain relief and fever control are important first. If symptoms fail to improve within 24–48 hours, oral antibiotics may be considered.
Answer Context: Initial management of uncomplicated acute otitis media includes analgesia and antipyretics. Antibiotics may be considered when symptoms fail to improve within 24–48 hours. First-line treatment may include amoxicillin, with escalation to co-amoxiclav if there is no improvement.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Acute Otitis Media > Treatment; revised PDF p. 71
Figure Captions: Figure 30: Bulging tympanic membrane in AOM (Courtesy of Elef ENT).

Question: Why are doctors concerned about complications from an ear infection?
Answer: Most ear infections settle, but complications can affect the eardrum, hearing, mastoid bone, inner ear, facial nerve, or even spread inside the skull.
Answer Context: Acute otitis media can lead to intratemporal complications such as tympanosclerosis, hearing loss, tympanic membrane perforation, mastoiditis, labyrinthitis, and facial nerve palsy. Intracranial complications include meningitis, intracranial abscess, lateral sinus thrombosis, cavernous sinus thrombosis, and subdural empyema.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Acute Otitis Media > Complications; revised PDF p. 70
Figure Captions: Figure 30: Bulging tympanic membrane in AOM (Courtesy of Elef ENT).

Question: What is glue ear, and is it the same as an ear infection?
Answer: Glue ear is fluid behind the eardrum without signs of infection. It is different from acute otitis media and often improves on its own.
Answer Context: Otitis media with effusion is a non-infective middle-ear effusion behind the tympanic membrane. It may follow acute otitis media but is considered a separate condition. In children, Eustachian tube anatomy predisposes to poor ventilation, and about half of cases resolve spontaneously within three months.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Media with Effusion > Description and epidemiology; revised PDF p. 72
Figure Captions: Figure 31: Dull right tympanic membrane with visible fluid level (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why might glue ear affect my child’s speech or behaviour?
Answer: Fluid behind the eardrum can cause conductive hearing loss. In young children, reduced hearing may affect speech development, attention, and behaviour.
Answer Context: Middle-ear effusion reduces tympanic membrane compliance and impairs sound conduction. Infants may be asymptomatic, but parents may notice hearing difficulty, behavioural problems, or delayed speech. Audiometry can show conductive hearing loss, and tympanometry often shows a flat type B trace.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Media with Effusion > Symptoms, signs, and investigations; revised PDF pp. 72–73
Figure Captions: Figure 31: Dull right tympanic membrane with visible fluid level (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why is one-sided glue ear in an adult treated more cautiously?
Answer: In adults, a one-sided middle-ear effusion can rarely be caused by a tumour blocking the Eustachian tube opening, so the back of the nose should be examined.
Answer Context: Adult unilateral middle-ear effusion requires exclusion of nasopharyngeal obstruction. A nasopharyngeal tumour may block Eustachian tube drainage and cause persistent effusion. Flexible nasoendoscopy is therefore recommended to assess the post-nasal space and Eustachian tube region.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Otitis Media with Effusion > Adult unilateral effusion; revised PDF pp. 72–73
Figure Captions: Figure 31: Dull right tympanic membrane with visible fluid level (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: What does it mean if I have a long-term hole in my eardrum with repeated discharge?
Answer: This can fit mucosal chronic otitis media, especially if there is a tympanic membrane perforation with recurrent or persistent infection.
Answer Context: Chronic otitis media includes mucosal disease, where a tympanic membrane perforation coexists with recurrent or persistent middle-ear infection. Disease may be inactive, with a dry perforation, or active, with a wet perforation, inflamed middle-ear mucosa, otorrhoea, and possible secondary otitis externa.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Chronic Otitis Media > Mucosal chronic otitis media; revised PDF pp. 75–76
Figure Captions: Figure 32: Right TM perforation (Courtesy of Elef-ENT)

Question: Why might surgery be offered for a chronic eardrum perforation?
Answer: Repairing the eardrum may help prevent recurrent infections and make the ear safer. Hearing may improve, but the main aim is often to stop repeated infection.
Answer Context: Myringoplasty, also called type 1 tympanoplasty, repairs tympanic membrane perforation using cartilage or fascia. It is indicated to prevent recurrent otitis media and restore the barrier between the external canal and middle ear. Treatment of active discharge also includes aural toilet and antibiotic-steroid drops when infection is present.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Chronic Otitis Media > Treatment; revised PDF p. 76
Figure Captions: Figure 32: Right TM perforation (Courtesy of Elef-ENT)

Question: My doctor said “cholesteatoma,” but is it cancer?
Answer: No. A cholesteatoma is not a cancer and does not consist of cholesterol. It is a destructive collection of skin-like cells in the wrong place, usually in the middle ear.
Answer Context: Cholesteatoma is an accumulation of benign keratinising squamous cells, most commonly involving the middle ear. Although non-malignant, it can locally destroy adjacent structures because the cells are hyperproliferative and enzymatically active. It is often described clinically as skin in the wrong place.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Cholesteatoma > Description; revised PDF p. 77
Figure Captions: Figure 33: Active squamous chronic otitis media in left ear: attic retraction with retained squamous epithelial debris (cholesteatoma) (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why does a smelly ear discharge that keeps coming back worry the ENT doctor?
Answer: Persistent or recurrent offensive discharge with one-sided hearing loss can suggest cholesteatoma, especially if it does not settle with topical antibiotics.
Answer Context: Acquired cholesteatoma often develops from Eustachian tube dysfunction that causes tympanic membrane retraction. When the retraction becomes deep enough, normal keratin migration fails and trapped keratin accumulates. Infection of the debris leads to chronic discharge, and progressive local erosion can damage middle-ear structures.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Cholesteatoma > Causes, symptoms, and signs; revised PDF pp. 77–78
Figure Captions: Figure 33: Active squamous chronic otitis media in left ear: attic retraction with retained squamous epithelial debris (cholesteatoma) (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why is surgery usually recommended for cholesteatoma?
Answer: Surgery is usually needed because cholesteatoma can keep growing, cause recurrent discharge, damage nearby bone or hearing bones, and lead to serious complications.
Answer Context: Cholesteatoma is generally managed surgically unless the patient is not fit for surgery. The aim is to remove the cholesteatoma sac, repair the tympanic membrane and adjacent bony defects, prevent recurrent discharge, and reconstruct hearing when ossicular destruction has occurred. CT of the temporal bone helps assess disease extent.
Handbook Reference: Otology > Cholesteatoma > Investigations and treatment; revised PDF p. 79
Figure Captions: Figure 33: Active squamous chronic otitis media in left ear: attic retraction with retained squamous epithelial debris (cholesteatoma) (Courtesy of Elef-ENT).

Question: Why does my nose matter so much for breathing if I can also breathe through my mouth?
Answer: The nose does more than let air pass through. It warms, humidifies, filters, and conditions the air before it reaches the lower airway, so persistent nasal blockage can affect comfort, sleep, smell, and breathing quality.
Answer Context: The primary function of the nose is ventilation, but nasal physiology also includes humidification, temperature regulation, filtration by nasal hairs, mucociliary clearance, immune protection, olfaction, middle-ear ventilation through the Eustachian tube, nasolacrimal drainage, and contribution to voice resonance.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal Function and Anatomy > Nasal function; revised PDF pp. 81–82
Figure Captions: Figure 35: Bony and cartilaginous septum. (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com). || Figure 36: Left Inferior turbinate as viewed with the rigid endoscope.

Question: Why can a bent septum make one side of my nose feel blocked?
Answer: The septum divides the two nasal passages. If it is significantly deviated, especially near the front of the nose, it can narrow one airway and cause obstruction.
Answer Context: The nasal septum partitions the nasal cavity and is formed by cartilaginous and bony structures including the maxillary crest, perpendicular plate of the ethmoid, and vomer. Septal deviation is common, but anterior or marked deviation can become clinically significant by reducing nasal airflow.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal Function and Anatomy > Nasal cavity and septum; revised PDF pp. 82–83
Figure Captions: Figure 35: Bony and cartilaginous septum. (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why do the turbinates in my nose swell and make breathing harder?
Answer: Turbinates contain tissue that can engorge in response to allergy, irritation, infection, temperature change, or other triggers. When swollen, they can narrow the nasal airway.
Answer Context: The lateral nasal wall contains turbinates covered by respiratory epithelium and containing venous plexuses. These tissues participate in humidifying and filtering inspired air, but their capacity to engorge under neural, hormonal, allergic, or inflammatory influence can contribute to nasal obstruction.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal Function and Anatomy > Turbinates; revised PDF pp. 83–84
Figure Captions: Figure 36: Left Inferior turbinate as viewed with the rigid endoscope.

Question: Is a nosebleed usually harmless, or can it be serious?
Answer: Many nosebleeds are minor, but a heavy or ongoing nosebleed can be serious and occasionally life-threatening. Active significant bleeding should be assessed where resuscitation support is available.
Answer Context: Epistaxis ranges from mild anterior bleeding to potentially life-threatening haemorrhage. Assessment must consider haemodynamic stability, blood loss, comorbid disease, anticoagulant use, and bleeding source. Active bleeding may require simultaneous focused history-taking, resuscitation, intravenous access, blood tests, and fluid support.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Epistaxis > Initial assessment and urgency; revised PDF p. 85

Question: Why does the doctor ask which side the nosebleed started from?
Answer: The starting side can help identify the likely bleeding point. It also helps guide examination and management, especially if bleeding keeps recurring from the same side.
Answer Context: In epistaxis history-taking, unilateral predominance helps localise the likely source. Whether blood first exits the nostril or runs down the throat may suggest anterior or posterior bleeding, which affects management strategy. Frequency, volume, comorbidities, anticoagulant use, trauma, and previous nasal surgery also influence risk assessment.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Epistaxis > History; revised PDF p. 85

Question: Why do so many nosebleeds come from the front part of the nose?
Answer: Many nosebleeds arise from Little’s area, a blood-vessel-rich region at the front of the nasal septum. Because it is exposed and vascular, it is a common bleeding site.
Answer Context: Little’s area, also called Kiesselbach’s plexus, lies on the anterior septum and contains an anastomotic network between branches of the internal and external carotid arterial systems. Its exposed anterior location makes it a frequent source of epistaxis, particularly in anterior bleeding.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Epistaxis > Examination and Little’s area; revised PDF p. 86

Question: When my nose bleeds, should I pinch the bridge of my nose or the soft part?
Answer: You should lean forward and pinch the soft part of the nose, not the hard bony bridge. This applies pressure to the septum, where many nosebleeds arise.
Answer Context: First-line mechanical control of anterior epistaxis involves leaning forward and compressing the soft cartilaginous part of the nose against the septum. Pinching the bony nasal bridge is ineffective because it does not compress the common anterior bleeding region. Cold measures may help by promoting vasoconstriction.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Epistaxis > Initial management; revised PDF p. 86
Figure Captions: Figure 37: Incorrect method of pinching the nose. || Figure 38: Correct method of pinching the nose.

Question: What happens if pressure and cautery do not stop a nosebleed?
Answer: If simple measures fail, or if the bleeding comes from further back in the nose, specialist treatment may be needed. This can include surgery to control the feeding artery.
Answer Context: Persistent or posterior epistaxis may require operative intervention. Endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation under general anaesthesia is used when posterior bleeding or failed conservative measures indicate a deeper vascular source. Traumatic anterior bleeding may involve the anterior ethmoidal artery and require a different surgical approach.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Epistaxis > Surgical management of epistaxis; revised PDF pp. 87–88
Figure Captions: Figure 39: An endoscopic clinical photograph of the right Sphenopalatine artery being clipped in the back of the nose under general anaesthetic for epistaxis. 2 clips have been applied to the main branch.

Question: Why can a blocked nose have so many different causes?
Answer: Nasal blockage can come from infection, allergy, injury, septal deviation, previous surgery, medication effects, inflammatory disease, or growths. A careful history and examination help narrow the cause.
Answer Context: Nasal obstruction is a symptom rather than a diagnosis. Common acquired causes include infectious rhinitis or rhinosinusitis, allergic inflammation, developmental septal or bony deviation, trauma, septal haematoma, septal perforation, post-surgical adhesions, rhinitis medicamentosa, medication side effects, cocaine-related injury, neoplastic masses, and systemic inflammatory disease.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal obstruction > Causes of nasal obstruction; revised PDF p. 89

Question: Can using decongestant sprays for too long make my nose more blocked?
Answer: Yes. Prolonged use of topical decongestants can cause rebound congestion, called rhinitis medicamentosa. These sprays should usually be used only for short periods.
Answer Context: Chronic topical decongestant use can produce rhinitis medicamentosa, where pharmacological vasoconstriction is followed by rebound mucosal congestion. This creates a cycle of repeated spray use and worsening obstruction. Drug-related nasal obstruction may also arise from other medications or substance misuse.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal obstruction > Drug-related causes; revised PDF p. 89

Question: Why is one-sided nasal blockage sometimes treated more cautiously than both-sided blockage?
Answer: One-sided blockage may still be due to a simple structural or inflammatory cause, but clinicians are more cautious because a unilateral mass or tumour must not be missed.
Answer Context: Bilateral obstruction commonly reflects inflammatory or allergic disease, but unilateral obstruction raises a broader differential that includes septal deviation, antrochoanal polyp, benign tumour, or malignancy. Persistent unilateral obstruction, especially with bleeding, pain, eye symptoms, glue ear, weight loss, or neck lumps, requires careful assessment.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal obstruction and Other Sinonasal Lesions > Unilateral obstruction; revised PDF pp. 89, 111–113
Figure Captions: Figure 48: Inverted papilloma right nasal cavity arising from lateral wall of nose. || Figure 49: SCC of the Left Maxillary Sinus. || Figure 50: Endoscopic image of SCC Left Maxillary Sinus

Question: Why did the doctor say I do not need an X-ray for my broken nose?
Answer: Nasal fractures are usually diagnosed by history and examination rather than X-ray. The key issues are whether there is a septal haematoma, ongoing bleeding, new obstruction, or visible deformity.
Answer Context: Plain X-rays are not routinely required for nasal fractures because management is guided mainly by clinical assessment. The clinician must exclude septal haematoma, evaluate epistaxis, assess new nasal obstruction, and determine whether swelling obscures deformity requiring delayed review.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Fractured Nose > Assessment; revised PDF p. 90

Question: What is a septal haematoma, and why is it urgent?
Answer: A septal haematoma is a blood collection in the nasal septum after injury. It needs urgent treatment because it can damage the septal cartilage and lead to a saddle-shaped nose deformity.
Answer Context: Septal haematoma separates septal cartilage from its nutritional support, compromising oxygen supply and causing ischaemic cartilage necrosis if not drained promptly. Untreated cartilage loss can lead to collapse of nasal support and saddle nose deformity, so it is a critical finding after nasal trauma.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Fractured Nose > Septal haematoma; revised PDF pp. 88, 90
Figure Captions: Figure 40: Septal Haematoma after nasal trauma. This requires urgent drainage to avoid complications such as abscess and septal cartilage necrosis.

Question: Why am I being asked to come back after the swelling settles?
Answer: Swelling and bruising can hide whether the nose is actually deformed. Review after several days allows the clinician to judge the shape more accurately and discuss whether manipulation is needed.
Answer Context: After nasal trauma, swelling may obscure nasal vault deformity. If the patient is otherwise stable and there is no septal haematoma or uncontrolled bleeding, reassessment after about 5–7 days allows better evaluation of residual deformity and suitability for manipulation under anaesthesia.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Fractured Nose > Delayed review; revised PDF p. 90
Figure Captions: Figure 41: Pre-operative image of trauma resulting in significant nasal deformity.

Question: What does manipulation under anaesthetic mean for a broken nose?
Answer: It means the nasal bones are moved back toward the midline under anaesthesia. It may improve the shape, but risks include pain, bleeding, bruising, packing, splinting, and failure to restore the original appearance.
Answer Context: Manipulation under anaesthesia is considered when nasal bone displacement causes deformity and the injury is still within the reducible window. Consent should cover pain, bleeding, need for nasal packing, external splint use, bruising, and the possibility that the nose may not return fully to its previous shape.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Fractured Nose > Manipulation under anaesthesia; revised PDF pp. 90–92
Figure Captions: Figure 41: Pre-operative image of trauma resulting in significant nasal deformity.

Question: What is a septoplasty meant to fix?
Answer: Septoplasty is used to remodel a deviated nasal septum when it is contributing to functional nasal obstruction. It may also be done to improve access for some endoscopic nasal procedures.
Answer Context: Septoplasty addresses deviation of the cartilaginous or bony septum to improve the nasal airway. It is commonly performed under general anaesthesia as a day-case procedure and may also facilitate endoscopic access for epistaxis surgery or provide septal cartilage for grafting.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Septoplasty/Septorhinoplasty > Septoplasty; revised PDF p. 93

Question: Why would someone need septorhinoplasty instead of just septoplasty?
Answer: Septorhinoplasty may be considered when both the septum and the external bony nasal framework are deviated. It can have both functional and cosmetic implications, so careful counselling is important.
Answer Context: Septorhinoplasty is broader than septoplasty because it can address deviation of the septum and the nasal bony vault. It may be performed externally through a columellar incision or endonasally. After trauma, corrective septoplasty or septorhinoplasty is usually delayed and planned electively rather than performed acutely.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Septoplasty/Septorhinoplasty > Septorhinoplasty; revised PDF p. 93

Question: What is the difference between rhinitis and sinusitis?
Answer: Rhinitis mainly means inflammation of the lining of the nose. Rhinosinusitis means inflammation involving both the nose and the paranasal sinuses.
Answer Context: Rhinitis is inflammation of the nasal mucous membranes and is characterised by nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, sneezing, itching, and post-nasal drip. Rhinosinusitis involves inflammatory disease of the nasal lining and paranasal sinuses, producing symptoms such as discharge, obstruction, facial pressure, and reduced smell.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Rhinitis and Rhinosinusitis > Description; revised PDF p. 94

Question: Why do blocked nose, runny nose, face pressure, and reduced smell often come together?
Answer: These symptoms can occur together because the nose and sinuses share a continuous lining. Inflammation can affect airflow, mucus drainage, smell, and pressure sensation at the same time.
Answer Context: Nasal and sinus mucosa function as a connected inflammatory system. When inflamed, mucosal swelling narrows the airway and sinus drainage pathways, mucus production increases, secretions may drain anteriorly or posteriorly, olfactory airflow can be reduced, and sinus pressure may be perceived as facial discomfort.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Rhinitis and Rhinosinusitis > Common symptoms; revised PDF p. 94

Question: Is hay fever actually an allergy inside the nose?
Answer: Yes. Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated type 1 hypersensitivity reaction of the nasal lining, often triggered by pollen, dust mites, mould, cats, or dogs.
Answer Context: Allergic rhinitis is an immune-mediated inflammatory response of the nasal mucosa. Intermittent disease may be triggered by seasonal allergens such as grass or tree pollen, while persistent symptoms may relate to house dust mite, mould, or animal dander exposure.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Allergic Rhinitis > Description and causes; revised PDF p. 95

Question: Why do my blocked nose and sneezing get worse around spring and summer?
Answer: Seasonal pollen exposure can trigger allergic rhinitis. Grass and tree pollens commonly cause intermittent symptoms such as sneezing, runny nose, nasal itching, and eye watering.
Answer Context: Intermittent allergic rhinitis is often pollen-driven and classically occurs during late spring or summer. Symptoms include seasonal rhinorrhoea, nasal itching, sneezing, nasal obstruction, and ocular symptoms. Examination may show pale or bluish turbinate mucosa, watery discharge, and turbinate enlargement.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Allergic Rhinitis > Seasonal allergic symptoms and signs; revised PDF pp. 95–96

Question: Why did the doctor ask whether I have asthma or eczema?
Answer: Allergic rhinitis is commonly associated with other atopic conditions, including asthma and eczema. Knowing this helps the clinician understand the allergic pattern and choose appropriate treatment.
Answer Context: Allergic rhinitis frequently coexists with atopic disease. A family history may be present, and approximately one third of patients with allergic rhinitis may also have asthma. This association is clinically relevant because medications such as leukotriene antagonists may be especially useful when asthma is also present.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Allergic Rhinitis > Epidemiology and associated atopy; revised PDF p. 95

Question: Why do I need to stop antihistamines before an allergy skin test?
Answer: Antihistamines can suppress the skin reaction and make the test less reliable. Stopping them beforehand helps avoid a falsely negative result.
Answer Context: Skin-prick testing introduces controls and allergen solutions into the dermis, then looks for a wheal response after about 15–20 minutes. Antihistamines can mask this histamine-mediated reaction, so they are usually avoided before testing. Contraindications include severe eczema, dermatographism, or previous anaphylaxis risk.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Allergic Rhinitis > Skin-prick testing; revised PDF pp. 96–98
Figure Captions: Figure 42: Skin Prick Allergy Testing (SPT) Droplets of various allergen solutions are placed onto the forearm and the skin is punctured. || Figure 43: After 15-20 minutes the results can be shown by the positive wheal reaction

Question: Why are nasal steroid sprays often recommended for allergic rhinitis?
Answer: Nasal steroid sprays reduce inflammation in the nasal lining and can help with blockage, runny nose, sneezing, and irritation. They usually need regular use for several weeks to work properly.
Answer Context: Allergic rhinitis treatment begins with allergen avoidance, nasal douching, barrier preparations, non-sedating oral antihistamines, and intranasal corticosteroid sprays. Steroid sprays are suitable for longer-term use if tolerated, while specialist escalation may include combination steroid-antihistamine sprays, leukotriene antagonists, or immunotherapy.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Allergic Rhinitis > Treatment; revised PDF pp. 98–99

Question: Can my nose be inflamed even if I do not have allergies?
Answer: Yes. Non-allergic rhinitis is inflammation of the nasal lining without an allergic cause. It can be triggered by smoke, pollution, cold dry air, spicy food, medicines, hormones, infection, or systemic disease.
Answer Context: Non-allergic rhinitis is common and may account for a large proportion of adult rhinitis. Causes include irritant exposure, vasomotor responses to temperature change, gustatory triggers, rhinitis medicamentosa, cocaine use, infection, granulomatous disease, sarcoidosis, hormonal causes, exercise, positional triggers, and atrophic rhinitis.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Non-Allergic Rhinitis > Causes; revised PDF p. 99

Question: Why does my nose run when I eat spicy food or go into cold air?
Answer: Some people have non-allergic nasal sensitivity where temperature changes or spicy foods trigger congestion or watery discharge. This is not necessarily an allergy.
Answer Context: Vasomotor and gustatory rhinitis are non-allergic forms of nasal mucosal reactivity. Cold, dry air can trigger vasomotor congestion, while spicy food can provoke gustatory rhinorrhoea. These patterns are inflammatory or neurovascular rather than IgE-mediated allergic reactions.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Non-Allergic Rhinitis > Vasomotor and gustatory causes; revised PDF p. 99

Question: Why would surgery not completely cure non-allergic rhinitis?
Answer: The underlying problem is usually in the nasal lining, not only in the size of the nasal passages. Surgery may improve airflow or medication delivery, but nasal sprays or rinses may still be needed.
Answer Context: Non-allergic rhinitis is primarily a mucosal disorder, so non-surgical management is the mainstay. Trigger avoidance, smoking cessation, saline douching, intranasal steroids, ipratropium, and short-term decongestants may help. Turbinate surgery can improve obstruction and topical drug access but is not usually curative.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Non-Allergic Rhinitis > Treatment; revised PDF pp. 99–100

Question: Is acute sinusitis usually caused by bacteria?
Answer: Most acute rhinosinusitis is viral, like a common cold. Bacterial sinusitis is less common, so antibiotics are usually reserved for more persistent, worsening, or severe cases.
Answer Context: Acute rhinosinusitis is symptomatic inflammation of the nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses lasting less than 12 weeks. Most cases are viral, caused by common respiratory viruses. Only a minority of upper respiratory infections progress to acute bacterial sinusitis, so antibiotic use should be selective.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Acute Rhinosinusitis > Description, epidemiology, and causes; revised PDF p. 101
Figure Captions: Figure 44: Mucopurulent discharge from the Right Middle Meatus (Sinus Drainage pathway) may be indicative of bacterial infection. (NS) Nasal septum, (MT)Middle Turbinate, (IT) Inferior Turbinate.

Question: What symptoms make sinusitis more likely than just a mild cold?
Answer: Nasal blockage with coloured discharge, facial pain or pressure, reduced smell, fever, malaise, and worsening after an initially mild illness can suggest acute rhinosinusitis.
Answer Context: Diagnostic symptom patterns for acute rhinosinusitis include sudden onset of at least two symptoms, one of which should be nasal obstruction or nasal discharge. Facial pain or pressure and reduced smell may occur. “Double sickening,” where symptoms worsen after initial improvement, suggests post-viral or bacterial disease.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Acute Rhinosinusitis > Symptoms; revised PDF pp. 101–102
Figure Captions: Figure 44: Mucopurulent discharge from the Right Middle Meatus (Sinus Drainage pathway) may be indicative of bacterial infection. (NS) Nasal septum, (MT)Middle Turbinate, (IT) Inferior Turbinate.

Question: Why is a painful swollen eye during sinusitis treated as urgent?
Answer: Infection from the sinuses can rarely spread around the eye. This can threaten vision, so eye swelling, proptosis, double vision, or reduced vision needs urgent assessment.
Answer Context: Complicated acute rhinosinusitis can spread extracranially to cause pre-septal cellulitis, orbital cellulitis, or orbital abscess. Intra-orbital sepsis can threaten vision by increasing pressure around the optic nerve. Severe headache, vomiting, photophobia, or neurological symptoms raise concern for intracranial complications.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Acute Rhinosinusitis > Complications; revised PDF pp. 102–103
Figure Captions: Figure 45: Evidence of a sub-periosteal abscess secondary to sinusitis causing right eye proptosis, peri-orbital swelling, chemosis and loss of the pupillary reflexes. Urgent surgery is required to reduce pressure on the optic nerve.

Question: Why did the doctor say I may only need pain relief, fluids, saline rinses, and a short decongestant course?
Answer: Mild symptoms for less than five days are commonly managed supportively because many cases are viral and settle without antibiotics.
Answer Context: Mild acute rhinosinusitis of short duration is managed with analgesia, nasal saline irrigation, fluid rehydration, and a short course of nasal decongestant. If symptoms persist beyond 10 days or worsen after 5 days, topical intranasal steroids may be added; severe disease may require antibiotics.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Acute Rhinosinusitis > Treatment by duration and severity; revised PDF p. 104

Question: Why would a CT scan be ordered for sinusitis only in certain cases?
Answer: CT is usually reserved for complicated, severe, or non-responding cases, especially if there are eye or brain-related warning signs.
Answer Context: Routine imaging is not required for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis. High-definition CT with contrast of the sinuses and brain is considered when complications occur or symptoms fail to respond, particularly with suspected orbital abscess, intracranial spread, or severe systemic illness.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Acute Rhinosinusitis > Investigations; revised PDF pp. 103–104
Figure Captions: Figure 45: Evidence of a sub-periosteal abscess secondary to sinusitis causing right eye proptosis, peri-orbital swelling, chemosis and loss of the pupillary reflexes. Urgent surgery is required to reduce pressure on the optic nerve. || Figure 46: CT Scan in coronal section showing bilateral maxillary sinusitis. R = Right side. Disease is worse on the left hand side mostly in the maxillary sinus and there is hypertrophy of the left inferior turbinate (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: When does sinus trouble become chronic rather than acute?
Answer: Rhinosinusitis is considered chronic when inflammation of the nose and sinuses lasts longer than 12 weeks. It may occur with or without nasal polyps.
Answer Context: Chronic rhinosinusitis is inflammation of the nasal mucosa and paranasal sinuses persisting for more than 12 weeks. It is commonly divided into disease with nasal polyps and disease without nasal polyps. Causes are multifactorial and may include allergic, occupational, hormonal, inflammatory, infective, anatomical, iatrogenic, or medication-related factors.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Chronic Rhinosinusitis > Description and causes; revised PDF p. 105
Figure Captions: Figure 46: CT Scan in coronal section showing bilateral maxillary sinusitis. R = Right side. Disease is worse on the left hand side mostly in the maxillary sinus and there is hypertrophy of the left inferior turbinate (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why do doctors use both symptoms and camera or CT findings to diagnose chronic sinus disease?
Answer: Symptoms show how the condition affects you, but endoscopy or CT helps confirm inflammation, polyps, swelling, pus, or sinus changes.
