Answer Context: Diagnostic criteria for chronic rhinosinusitis require two or more symptoms such as nasal blockage, discharge, facial pressure, or reduced smell, together with objective evidence. Objective evidence may include endoscopic polyps, middle meatal oedema, mucopurulent discharge, or CT mucosal changes in the ostiomeatal complex or sinuses.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Chronic Rhinosinusitis / Nasal Polyps > Diagnostic criteria; revised PDF pp. 105–106
Figure Captions: Figure 46: CT Scan in coronal section showing bilateral maxillary sinusitis. R = Right side. Disease is worse on the left hand side mostly in the maxillary sinus and there is hypertrophy of the left inferior turbinate (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Are nasal polyps cancer?
Answer: Typical inflammatory nasal polyps are benign and often occur on both sides. However, a one-sided polyp-like mass needs careful assessment because other lesions can mimic a polyp.
Answer Context: Nasal polyps are common benign inflammatory lesions, usually bilateral, grey, oedematous, and associated with chronic rhinosinusitis. They commonly cause nasal blockage and altered smell. A unilateral polyp-like lesion should prompt caution because antrochoanal polyps, inverted papilloma, skull-base lesions, or malignancy can present differently.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal Polyps > Description; revised PDF pp. 108–109
Figure Captions: Figure 47: Clinical photograph of left nasal cavity showing inflammatory nasal polyps arising from the sinus drainage pathway (Middle Meatus) blocking nasal airway.

Question: Why might nasal polyps come back even after surgery?
Answer: Surgery can remove polyps and reopen drainage pathways, but it does not always cure the underlying inflammatory tendency. Ongoing nasal rinses and steroid treatment may still be needed.
Answer Context: Medical management of nasal polyps commonly combines saline irrigation and steroid-based therapy. Surgery, including endoscopic polypectomy or functional endoscopic sinus surgery, improves access, removes obstructive tissue, and restores drainage pathways when medical treatment fails. Recurrence remains possible because the inflammatory disease process can persist.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Nasal Polyps > Medical and surgical treatment; revised PDF pp. 109–110
Figure Captions: Figure 47: Clinical photograph of left nasal cavity showing inflammatory nasal polyps arising from the sinus drainage pathway (Middle Meatus) blocking nasal airway.

Question: Should I worry if a doctor says something looks like a one-sided nasal polyp?
Answer: It needs proper assessment. Some one-sided polyp-like lesions are benign, but others can be locally aggressive, vascular, connected to the skull base, or rarely malignant.
Answer Context: Unilateral sinonasal lesions have a broader differential than typical bilateral inflammatory polyps. Inverted papilloma is benign but locally aggressive and recurrent if incompletely removed. Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is a vascular tumour of adolescent males. Meningoencephalocoele or glioma requires MRI before biopsy because of possible skull-base connection.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Other Sinonasal Lesions > Benign lesions; revised PDF pp. 111–112
Figure Captions: Figure 48: Inverted papilloma right nasal cavity arising from lateral wall of nose.

Question: What symptoms could suggest a more serious sinus or nasal tumour?
Answer: Persistent one-sided blockage, bleeding, pain, one-sided glue ear, neck lumps, eye symptoms, headaches, weight loss, or nerve symptoms should be assessed carefully.
Answer Context: Sinonasal malignancies are rare but may present late. Warning symptoms include unilateral nasal obstruction, unilateral glue ear, epistaxis, pain, cervical lymphadenopathy, unexplained weight loss, orbital symptoms, headaches, or cranial nerve deficits. Evaluation requires imaging and histology, with management planned through a multidisciplinary team.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Other Sinonasal Lesions > Malignant lesions; revised PDF pp. 112–113
Figure Captions: Figure 49: SCC of the Left Maxillary Sinus. || Figure 50: Endoscopic image of SCC Left Maxillary Sinus

Question: What does it mean when a baby is born with a cleft lip or palate?
Answer: A cleft is a gap in the upper lip and/or roof of the mouth. It can create an abnormal connection between the mouth and nose and may affect feeding, hearing, speech, teeth, and appearance.
Answer Context: Cleft lip and palate is a congenital separation of the upper lip and/or palate, creating abnormal communication between the oral and nasal cavities. Severity ranges from a relatively subtle submucous cleft to bilateral cleft lip and palate with immediate airway and feeding concerns.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Cleft Lip and Palate > Description; revised PDF p. 114
Figure Captions: Figure 51: Unilateral left cleft lip and palate (Courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why can a baby with a cleft palate struggle with feeding?
Answer: A cleft palate can make it difficult to create suction. Feeding may take longer, milk may come through the nose, and the baby may swallow excess air or tire easily.
Answer Context: Feeding difficulty in cleft palate arises from impaired separation of oral and nasal cavities and poor suction mechanics. Nasal regurgitation, prolonged feeds, excess air intake, poor airway protection, and high energy expenditure can contribute to poor weight gain. Specialised bottles and multidisciplinary support can improve feeding efficiency.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Cleft Lip and Palate > Feeding considerations; revised PDF pp. 114–115
Figure Captions: Figure 51: Unilateral left cleft lip and palate (Courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why does a child with cleft palate need hearing and speech checks?
Answer: Cleft palate can affect Eustachian tube function and increase the risk of glue ear. It can also affect speech and swallowing, so hearing assessment and speech therapy are important.
Answer Context: Abnormally developed palate muscles can impair Eustachian tube function, predisposing children with cleft palate to middle-ear effusion and delayed resolution of glue ear. Early hearing assessment, possible grommets or hearing aids, and speech and language therapy are important parts of multidisciplinary care.
Handbook Reference: Rhinology > Cleft Lip and Palate > Otologic and speech considerations; revised PDF pp. 114–115
Figure Captions: Figure 51: Unilateral left cleft lip and palate (Courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why would the doctor ask me to smile or puff my cheeks when checking a lump near my jaw?
Answer: These movements help assess the facial nerve. A lump near the parotid gland can sometimes affect this nerve, so facial movement gives important information.
Answer Context: The parotid gland lies near the ear and is anatomically related to the facial nerve. Facial expression movements such as smiling, cheek puffing, and eye closure help identify facial nerve dysfunction. In the context of a salivary gland mass, facial weakness is clinically significant because it can suggest malignant involvement.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Head and Neck Anatomy > Facial Muscles and Major Salivary Glands; revised PDF pp. 118–120

Question: Why does the doctor look inside my mouth when I complain about a neck lump?
Answer: The mouth and throat can be sources of infection, inflammation, or cancer that may cause lymph nodes in the neck to enlarge. Looking inside the mouth helps search for the cause.
Answer Context: The oral cavity includes the lips, floor of mouth, tongue, alveolar ridges, hard palate, and buccal lining. Disease in these areas can drain to cervical lymph nodes and present as a neck lump. Dental disease may also lead to submandibular or deep neck space infection.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Head and Neck Anatomy > Oral Cavity; revised PDF pp. 121–123
Figure Captions: Figure 52: Oral cavity (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why does the doctor describe neck lumps by “levels”?
Answer: Neck levels give clinicians a standard way to describe where a lump or lymph node is located. This helps guide possible causes, imaging, referrals, and treatment planning.
Answer Context: The neck is divided into anatomical levels using reproducible boundaries. These levels are particularly useful when documenting and communicating the site of lymph nodes or masses. Location can suggest likely pathology, such as salivary gland disease, dental infection, thyroid disease, branchial cyst, or metastatic lymphadenopathy.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Head and Neck Anatomy > Topography of the Neck; revised PDF pp. 124–125
Figure Captions: Figure 53: Levels of the neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why does the doctor feel under my jaw and along the side of my neck in a fixed order?
Answer: A systematic order helps ensure no important lymph node area or gland is missed. Different parts of the neck contain different structures and drainage areas.
Answer Context: Head and neck examination uses anatomical triangles and levels to organise palpation. The anterior and posterior triangles contain lymph nodes, salivary glands, vascular structures, thyroid-related structures, and muscular boundaries. A systematic approach improves detection and localisation of clinically meaningful masses.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Head and Neck Anatomy > Neck triangles and levels; revised PDF pp. 124–125
Figure Captions: Figure 53: Levels of the neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why can neck surgery affect my voice, shoulder, or tongue movement?
Answer: Several important nerves pass through the head and neck. Depending on the operation or disease site, nerves controlling the voice box, shoulder, tongue, or swallowing muscles may be at risk.
Answer Context: Important head and neck nerves include the recurrent laryngeal nerve, spinal accessory nerve, hypoglossal nerve, glossopharyngeal nerve, lingual nerve, and others. Injury to these nerves may cause vocal cord paralysis, shoulder weakness, tongue deviation, taste or sensation changes, or swallowing-related problems.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Head and Neck Anatomy > Nervous Supply of the Head and Neck; revised PDF pp. 128–132

Question: Why does swallowing feel simple even though doctors say it is complex?
Answer: Swallowing feels automatic, but it involves coordinated mouth, throat, voice box, and oesophageal movements. These protect the airway while moving food safely toward the stomach.
Answer Context: Swallowing has oral, pharyngeal, and oesophageal stages. The oral phase prepares and propels the bolus. The pharyngeal phase closes the nasopharynx, elevates and protects the larynx, and drives the bolus downward. The oesophageal phase uses sphincter relaxation and peristalsis to move the bolus toward the stomach.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Physiology of Swallowing; revised PDF pp. 133–134
Figure Captions: Figure 57: Swallowing Physiology

Question: Why is coughing or choking during meals important to mention?
Answer: Coughing during meals may mean food or liquid is entering or threatening the airway. This can increase the risk of aspiration and chest infections.
Answer Context: Safe swallowing depends on laryngeal closure, laryngeal elevation, and coordinated pharyngeal contraction. If airway protection fails, swallowed material may enter the larynx or lower airway. This can cause coughing, choking, aspiration, and recurrent chest infections, especially in neurological or structural swallowing disorders.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Physiology of Swallowing; revised PDF pp. 133–134
Figure Captions: Figure 57: Swallowing Physiology

Subsection: Taking History of a Neck Lump
Question: Why does the doctor ask so many questions about a lump in my neck?
Answer: The questions help determine whether the lump is likely inflammatory, congenital, salivary, thyroid-related, or possibly malignant. The duration, growth pattern, pain, and associated symptoms are especially important.
Answer Context: Neck lump history-taking assesses age, occupation, onset, number of lumps, site, size change, persistence, pain, swallowing difficulty, breathing difficulty, bleeding, weight change, ear pain, night sweats, fever, voice change, prior investigations, and prior treatment. These details refine the differential diagnosis and urgency of referral.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Taking History of a Neck Lump; revised PDF pp. 135–136

Subsection: Taking History of a Neck Lump
Question: Why does my doctor ask if I smoke or drink when I have a neck lump?
Answer: Smoking and alcohol increase the risk of some head and neck cancers. If a neck lump is present, these risk factors help decide how urgently it should be investigated.
Answer Context: Social history is essential in neck lump assessment because smoking and alcohol exposure are major risk factors for upper aerodigestive tract malignancy. A persistent neck mass with risk factors, especially when associated with hoarseness, swallowing symptoms, ear pain, weight loss, or bleeding, warrants careful evaluation.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Taking History of a Neck Lump > Social History; revised PDF p. 136

Subsection: Taking History of a Neck Lump
Question: Is a neck lump more worrying if it changes over several weeks?
Answer: Yes. An unexplained neck lump that changes or persists over several weeks should be assessed promptly, especially if there are voice, swallowing, breathing, bleeding, or weight-loss symptoms.
Answer Context: Red flags for urgent referral include an unexplained neck lump changing over 3–6 weeks, hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks, dysphagia, odynophagia, haemoptysis, unexplained weight loss, otalgia, night sweats, fever, or breathing difficulty. These features can indicate malignant or serious inflammatory disease.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Taking History of a Neck Lump > Red Flags; revised PDF p. 136

Question: Why does the position of my neck lump matter?
Answer: Different areas of the neck contain different glands, lymph nodes, and structures. The position helps suggest whether the cause may be dental, salivary, branchial, thyroid, or lymph-node related.
Answer Context: Neck lump localisation by anatomical level helps guide differential diagnosis. Level I suggests submandibular gland disease or tooth abscess; levels II and III may include parotid pathology, branchial cyst, or lymphadenopathy; level VI is commonly related to thyroid disease. Location must be interpreted with clinical history and examination.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Causes of Neck Lump; revised PDF p. 137
Figure Captions: Figure 53: Levels of the neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Can a neck lump just be an infection?
Answer: Yes. Enlarged lymph nodes can occur with infection or inflammation, but persistent, enlarging, hard, fixed, or unexplained lumps need proper assessment.
Answer Context: Cervical lymphadenopathy can be benign or malignant. Infection commonly causes tender, reactive nodes, but persistent, non-tender, enlarging, or fixed lymph nodes raise concern for malignancy. Clinical context, drainage area, constitutional symptoms, smoking and alcohol history, and imaging or cytology guide evaluation.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Causes of Neck Lump; revised PDF p. 137

Question: What is a branchial cyst, and why does it appear on the side of the neck?
Answer: A branchial cyst is a congenital-type cyst that often appears as an upper neck mass in young adults. It can become infected and may need imaging and surgical treatment.
Answer Context: Branchial cysts commonly present as upper neck masses in young adults, often in the third decade. They are epithelial inclusions within lymph nodes and may become infected. Evaluation commonly includes ultrasound, fine needle aspiration cytology, and cross-sectional imaging, with surgical excision as treatment.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Branchial Cysts; revised PDF p. 138

Question: What does “dysphagia” mean when the doctor writes it down?
Answer: Dysphagia means difficulty swallowing. The doctor will ask whether it affects solids, liquids, or saliva and where you feel food sticking.
Answer Context: Dysphagia is difficulty swallowing and may arise from pharyngeal, upper oesophageal, mid-oesophageal, or lower oesophageal pathology. Characterising whether solids, liquids, or saliva are affected helps distinguish mechanical obstruction from motility or neurological causes.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphagia > Description and History; revised PDF p. 139

Question: Why would ear pain matter if my main problem is swallowing?
Answer: Pain from the throat can be felt in the ear through shared nerve pathways. Ear pain with swallowing symptoms can be a warning sign that needs careful examination.
Answer Context: Associated symptoms in dysphagia include hoarseness, odynophagia, choking, regurgitation, nasal regurgitation, otalgia, weight loss, and aspiration. Referred otalgia can occur in pharyngeal or laryngeal pathology and is clinically important when evaluating possible upper aerodigestive tract malignancy.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphagia > Associated Symptoms; revised PDF p. 139

Question: Why might I need a barium swallow or camera test for swallowing problems?
Answer: These tests help show whether food passage is blocked, poorly coordinated, or affected by a pouch, stricture, tumour, or movement disorder.
Answer Context: Dysphagia investigations may include blood tests, chest radiography, CT or MRI when malignancy is suspected, barium swallow, panendoscopy with laryngoscopy and upper oesophagoscopy, and dynamic studies such as videofluoroscopy when motility disorders are suspected.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphagia > Investigations; revised PDF p. 140

Question: Can a neck mass or thyroid swelling make swallowing difficult?
Answer: Yes. A large neck mass, retrosternal goitre, or other external pressure can compress the throat or oesophagus and cause swallowing difficulty.
Answer Context: Extraluminal causes of dysphagia include external compression from neck masses, retrosternal goitre, vascular abnormalities, mediastinal masses, or lung cancer. These conditions narrow or distort the pharyngeal or oesophageal passage from outside the lumen.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphagia > Extraluminal Causes; revised PDF p. 140

Question: What does dysphonia mean?
Answer: Dysphonia means hoarseness or a change in voice quality, pitch, loudness, or effort. It can come from inflammation, vocal cord problems, nerve problems, trauma, or more serious causes.
Answer Context: Dysphonia is altered vocal quality, pitch, loudness, or vocal effort that impairs communication. Causes include malignant disease, benign vocal cord lesions, vocal cord palsy, trauma, endocrine disease, infection, iatrogenic recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, and functional muscle tension dysphonia.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphonia > Description and Causes; revised PDF p. 141

Question: When should I worry about a hoarse voice?
Answer: A hoarse voice that persists, worsens, or occurs with smoking history, alcohol use, neck lump, weight loss, coughing blood, swallowing pain, ear pain, or neurological symptoms needs prompt assessment.
Answer Context: Red flags in dysphonia include smoking and alcohol history, neck mass, unexplained weight loss, neurological symptoms, haemoptysis, dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, persistent worsening hoarseness, and immunocompromise. These features suggest the need for urgent ENT evaluation.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphonia > Red Flags; revised PDF p. 141

Question: Could my inhaler make my voice hoarse?
Answer: Yes. Inhaled corticosteroids can predispose some people to fungal irritation or laryngitis, which may contribute to hoarseness. Your clinician will consider this alongside other causes.
Answer Context: Infective causes of dysphonia include laryngitis and candida, with inhaled corticosteroid use noted as a predisposing factor. Medication history is therefore relevant when assessing hoarseness, especially if symptoms coincide with inhaler use or visible laryngeal irritation.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Dysphonia > Infective and Iatrogenic Causes; revised PDF p. 141

Question: Why might a hoarse voice after thyroid surgery be checked carefully?
Answer: The recurrent laryngeal nerve, which moves the vocal cord, can be affected by thyroid surgery. A voice change may need laryngeal examination.
Answer Context: The recurrent laryngeal nerve supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid. It is at risk during thyroid surgery, and injury can cause vocal cord paralysis. Postoperative hoarseness should therefore prompt consideration of vocal cord function and airway safety.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Nervous Supply / Dysphonia; revised PDF pp. 132, 141

Question: Is tonsillitis usually bacterial?
Answer: Not always. Most acute tonsillitis is viral, especially in children and young adults, so antibiotics are not useful for every sore throat.
Answer Context: Tonsillitis is infection of the palatine tonsils and may be viral or bacterial. Acute tonsillitis is common in children and young adults, with viral causes forming the majority and bacterial causes forming a smaller proportion. Group A beta-haemolytic streptococcus is the commonest bacterial cause.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Tonsillitis > Description and Epidemiology; revised PDF p. 142
Figure Captions: Figure 58: Acute Tonsillitis illustrating white follicles on the tonsils (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why does tonsillitis sometimes cause earache?
Answer: Throat pain can be referred to the ear through shared nerve pathways. This means the ear can hurt even when the main infection is in the tonsils.
Answer Context: Tonsillitis can cause odynophagia, dysphagia, earache, malaise, headache, fever, swollen tonsils, exudate, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Referred otalgia occurs because sensory innervation of the throat and ear overlaps through cranial nerve pathways.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Tonsillitis > Symptoms; revised PDF p. 142

Question: What is a “hot potato” voice in tonsillitis?
Answer: A muffled, thick voice can occur when the tonsils or tissues around them are swollen. If it occurs with severe one-sided pain, trismus, or uvula deviation, a quinsy may be suspected.
Answer Context: Tonsillar enlargement can produce a muffled or “hot potato” voice. In peritonsillar abscess, severe usually unilateral pain, trismus, uvular deviation away from the swelling, and peritonsillar swelling suggest pus collection around the tonsil rather than uncomplicated tonsillitis.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Tonsillitis > Signs and Natural History; revised PDF pp. 142–144
Figure Captions: Figure 58: Acute Tonsillitis illustrating white follicles on the tonsils (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com). || Figure 59: Left Sided Peritonsillar Abscess (Quinsy) (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why does the doctor use the Centor criteria before giving antibiotics?
Answer: The Centor criteria estimate whether tonsillitis is likely bacterial. Antibiotics are generally considered when enough features suggest bacterial infection.
Answer Context: Antibiotic decisions in acute tonsillitis may be guided by Centor criteria: tonsillar exudate, fever, absence of cough, and tender cervical lymph nodes. When three or four criteria are present, bacterial tonsillitis is more likely and antibiotics are more appropriate.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Tonsillitis > Antibiotics and Centor Criteria; revised PDF p. 144

Question: When is tonsil removal considered for repeated sore throats?
Answer: Tonsillectomy may be considered when sore throats are clearly due to acute tonsillitis, are disabling, and occur frequently over documented periods.
Answer Context: Recurrent acute sore throat may justify tonsillectomy when episodes are due to acute tonsillitis, significantly impair normal function, and meet frequency thresholds such as seven or more significant episodes in one year, five or more per year for two years, or three or more per year for three years.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Tonsillitis > Tonsillectomy Indications; revised PDF p. 145

Question: What is a pharyngeal pouch?
Answer: A pharyngeal pouch, also called Zenker’s diverticulum, is an outpouching from the throat wall near the upper oesophageal sphincter. It can trap food and cause swallowing problems.
Answer Context: A pharyngeal pouch is an outpouching of mucosa and submucosa through an area of weakness called Killian’s dehiscence between cricopharyngeus and thyropharyngeus. It is more common in elderly men and may relate to discoordination between sphincter opening and bolus propulsion.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Pharyngeal Pouch > Description and Cause; revised PDF p. 146
Figure Captions: Figure 60: Zenker's Diverticulum(reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why do I bring up undigested food hours after eating?
Answer: Regurgitation of undigested food can occur when food collects in a pharyngeal pouch and later comes back up. This can also cause bad breath and coughing.
Answer Context: A pharyngeal pouch can retain food in the hypopharyngeal outpouching. Symptoms include progressive dysphagia, lump sensation, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis from food stasis, gurgling, and recurrent chest infections if aspiration occurs.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Pharyngeal Pouch > Symptoms and Signs; revised PDF p. 146
Figure Captions: Figure 60: Zenker's Diverticulum(reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why is a barium swallow used for suspected pharyngeal pouch?
Answer: A barium swallow outlines the pouch and helps confirm the diagnosis. It shows how contrast moves through the throat and upper oesophagus.
Answer Context: Barium swallow is the definitive investigation for pharyngeal pouch because it demonstrates the outpouching and bolus pathway. Rigid oesophagoscopy may also be used to exclude carcinoma of the pouch wall. Symptomatic pouches may be treated by endoscopic stapling or open surgery.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Pharyngeal Pouch > Investigations and Management; revised PDF p. 147
Figure Captions: Figure 60: Zenker's Diverticulum(reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why do I feel a lump in my throat even though I can swallow normally?
Answer: This can be globus pharyngeus, a sensation of a lump or foreign body in the throat without an obvious structural cause. It is diagnosed only after other causes are excluded.
Answer Context: Globus pharyngeus is the sensation of a lump, discomfort, or foreign body in the throat without an obvious cause. It is common in ENT clinics and is linked with stress, anxiety, somatisation, laryngopharyngeal reflux, cricopharyngeal spasm, and oesophagitis.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Globus Pharyngeus > Description; revised PDF p. 148

Question: Why do I still need a throat camera if the doctor thinks it is globus?
Answer: The camera helps exclude other causes before calling it globus. This is especially important if there are risk factors like smoking, heavy alcohol use, or concerning symptoms.
Answer Context: Globus is a diagnosis of exclusion. Thorough ENT examination, including flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy, is used to rule out other causes. In patients with smoking or excess alcohol history, further tests such as barium swallow, CT, or upper GI endoscopy may be considered to exclude oesophageal pathology.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Globus Pharyngeus > Investigations; revised PDF p. 148

Question: Why is ultrasound usually the first test for a thyroid lump?
Answer: Ultrasound shows whether the lump looks benign or suspicious and checks nearby lymph nodes. It also helps decide whether a needle sample is needed.
Answer Context: Ultrasound of the neck is first-line imaging for thyroid masses. It risk-stratifies thyroid lesions, distinguishes solitary nodules from nodules within multinodular goitre, assesses malignant cervical lymphadenopathy, and guides fine needle aspiration cytology when suspicious features are present.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Investigations; revised PDF p. 149

Question: What ultrasound features make a thyroid nodule more suspicious?
Answer: Suspicious features include a solid dark-looking nodule, tiny calcifications, irregular edges, a taller-than-wide shape, or abnormal lymph nodes.
Answer Context: Suspicious thyroid ultrasound findings include solid hypoechoic nodules with microcalcifications, irregular margins, taller-than-wide shape, and lymphadenopathy. Ultrasound grades range from U2 benign to U5 malignant, while equivocal U3 and U4 lesions require further investigation.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Ultrasound Features; revised PDF p. 149

Question: Why can a needle test diagnose some thyroid cancers but not follicular cancer?
Answer: A needle sample looks at cells, but follicular cancer is diagnosed by seeing invasion through the capsule or blood vessels, which requires examining the whole nodule after surgery.
Answer Context: Fine needle aspiration cytology can diagnose papillary carcinoma, but it cannot reliably distinguish follicular adenoma from follicular carcinoma. Follicular carcinoma depends on histological evidence of capsular or vascular invasion, so diagnostic hemithyroidectomy may be required.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology; revised PDF p. 149

Question: Why does a midline neck lump move when I stick my tongue out?
Answer: That movement can suggest a thyroglossal cyst because it is connected to the embryological path of the thyroid from the tongue base.
Answer Context: A thyroglossal cyst is an epithelial remnant of the thyroglossal tract. It may occur anywhere between the tongue base and trachea, usually near the hyoid bone. It classically moves upward with tongue protrusion and swallowing because of its embryological relationship to thyroid descent.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Thyroglossal Cyst or Sinus; revised PDF pp. 150–151

Question: Why must doctors check for a normal thyroid before removing a thyroglossal cyst?
Answer: Rarely, the cyst may contain the only functioning thyroid tissue. Removing it without confirming a normal thyroid gland could cause hypothyroidism.
Answer Context: Thyroglossal cysts can be associated with ectopic thyroid tissue and may very rarely contain the only functioning thyroid tissue. Ultrasound, with or without fine needle aspiration, confirms anatomy and demonstrates the presence of a normal thyroid gland before surgical treatment.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Thyroglossal Cyst or Sinus > Investigations; revised PDF p. 151

Question: What is a multinodular goitre?
Answer: A multinodular goitre is an enlarged thyroid containing multiple nodules. It may be painless and asymptomatic, but large goitres can cause cosmetic or pressure symptoms.
Answer Context: Multinodular goitre is the commonest cause of goitre in the Western world. It usually occurs in euthyroid patients and may present as a visible or palpable neck lump, cosmetic deformity, multiple irregular nodules, or pressure symptoms such as breathlessness, orthopnoea, and dysphagia when large.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Multinodular Goitre; revised PDF p. 152

Question: Why would a thyroid swelling make breathing worse when lying down?
Answer: A large goitre can press on the airway or extend behind the breastbone. This can cause breathlessness, especially when lying flat.
Answer Context: Large multinodular goitres can cause mass effect, including tracheal compression or retrosternal extension. Symptoms may include breathlessness, orthopnoea, dysphagia, and cosmetic deformity. CT neck and chest and pulmonary function testing may be used when retrosternal extension or obstruction is suspected.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Multinodular Goitre > Pressure Symptoms; revised PDF pp. 152–153

Question: Does a thyroid lump always mean cancer?
Answer: No. Many thyroid lumps are benign. However, suspicious ultrasound features, abnormal lymph nodes, family history, radiation exposure, or certain cytology results may require specialist review.
Answer Context: Thyroid cancer is less common than benign thyroid nodules. Risk factors include radiation exposure and family history. Work-up includes ultrasound, possible fine needle aspiration, thyroid function tests, and cytological classification. Suspicious nodules are discussed by a thyroid multidisciplinary team.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Thyroid Cancer; revised PDF pp. 154–157

Question: Why does the thyroid team talk about THY1, THY2, THY3, THY4, and THY5?
Answer: These categories describe cytology results from a thyroid needle sample and estimate cancer risk. They help the team decide whether repeat sampling, monitoring, or surgery is needed.
Answer Context: Thyroid nodules are classified cytologically using THY categories. THY1 is non-diagnostic, THY2 is non-neoplastic, THY3 is a follicular lesion requiring surgical assessment of the nodule, THY4 is suspicious but not diagnostic, and THY5 is diagnostic of malignancy. Management is guided through multidisciplinary review.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > THY Classification; revised PDF p. 154

Question: Why is papillary thyroid cancer often treated with surgery even when survival is good?
Answer: Surgery removes the thyroid cancer, allows staging, and helps manage lymph-node disease. Further treatment is individualised depending on risk and tumour features.
Answer Context: Papillary thyroid cancer is the most common thyroid cancer and often has excellent prognosis. Management may include thyroid lobectomy or total thyroidectomy depending on tumour size and risk. Neck dissection may be considered when metastatic cervical lymph nodes are present, followed by selective use of radioiodine and thyroglobulin monitoring.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Papillary Thyroid Cancer; revised PDF pp. 155–156

Question: Why does medullary thyroid cancer need genetic and hormone-related tests?
Answer: Some medullary thyroid cancers are inherited and linked with other endocrine tumours. Tests such as calcitonin, CEA, RET genetic screening, and urine metanephrines help guide safe treatment.
Answer Context: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells and may be sporadic or inherited through syndromes such as familial medullary thyroid cancer, MEN 2A, or MEN 2B. Investigation includes ultrasound, FNA, calcitonin, carcinoembryonic antigen, RET screening, and assessment for phaeochromocytoma before surgery.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma; revised PDF pp. 156–157

Question: Why is a rapidly growing hard thyroid lump in an older person concerning?
Answer: A rapidly enlarging hard thyroid mass can indicate an aggressive thyroid cancer or lymphoma. It needs urgent specialist assessment and often tissue biopsy.
Answer Context: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is rare, aggressive, and tends to affect elderly patients, presenting over months as a rapidly enlarging bulky hard neck mass. Thyroid lymphoma can also present as a rapidly enlarging goitre with compressive symptoms, especially in association with Hashimoto’s thyroiditis.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Thyroid Masses > Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer and Thyroid Lymphoma; revised PDF p. 157

Subsection: Benign and Malignant Neoplasms of the Salivary Glands
Question: Is a painless lump near the parotid gland usually cancer?
Answer: Many parotid lumps are benign, especially pleomorphic adenoma. However, any salivary gland mass should be assessed because some features can suggest malignancy.
Answer Context: Salivary gland tumours account for a small proportion of head and neck neoplasms. Around 80% arise in the parotid, and most parotid tumours are benign. Submandibular and sublingual tumours are less common but have a higher malignancy rate.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Benign and Malignant Neoplasms of the Salivary Glands; revised PDF p. 158
Figure Captions: Figure 61: Pleomorphic adenoma of the parotid gland (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Subsection: Benign and Malignant Neoplasms of the Salivary Glands
Question: What signs make a salivary gland lump more suspicious?
Answer: Hardness, rapid growth, tenderness, skin ulceration, fixation to surrounding tissue, or facial weakness are concerning features that need specialist assessment.
Answer Context: Red flags in salivary gland neoplasms include hardness, rapid growth, tenderness, infiltration of surrounding structures, overlying skin ulceration, and facial weakness. Facial palsy is particularly important in parotid masses because the facial nerve passes through the gland.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Benign and Malignant Neoplasms of the Salivary Glands > Red Flags; revised PDF p. 158
Figure Captions: Figure 61: Pleomorphic adenoma of the parotid gland (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Subsection: Benign and Malignant Neoplasms of the Salivary Glands
Question: Why remove a benign pleomorphic adenoma if it is not cancer?
Answer: Pleomorphic adenomas can continue growing and have a risk of malignant transformation. Surgical removal is often recommended to prevent future problems.
Answer Context: Pleomorphic adenoma is the commonest benign salivary tumour and commonly arises in the parotid gland. It presents as a painless, slowly enlarging retromandibular lump and carries malignant transformation potential, which is why surgical excision is usually recommended.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Salivary Gland Tumours > Pleomorphic Adenoma; revised PDF p. 159
Figure Captions: Figure 61: Pleomorphic adenoma of the parotid gland (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Subsection: Benign and Malignant Neoplasms of the Salivary Glands
Question: Why is facial weakness mentioned so much with salivary gland cancer?
Answer: Facial weakness suggests that a tumour may be affecting the facial nerve. This is an important warning sign, especially with parotid gland disease.
Answer Context: Malignant salivary tumours may involve nerves directly or through perineural spread. Adenoid cystic carcinoma characteristically spreads along nerves and may involve the facial nerve. Facial weakness in a salivary gland mass therefore increases concern for malignancy and influences surgical planning.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Salivary Gland Tumours > Malignant Salivary Tumours; revised PDF pp. 158–160

Question: What mouth symptoms should make me see a doctor rather than waiting?
Answer: A persistent ulcer, unexplained bleeding, mouth pain, loose teeth, white or red patches, or a lump in the mouth should be checked, especially if you smoke or drink alcohol.
Answer Context: Oral cavity carcinoma can affect the lips, tongue, floor of mouth, alveolar ridge, hard palate, or buccal lining. Most are squamous cell carcinomas. Symptoms may include persistent ulceration, pain, bleeding, loose teeth, leukoplakia, erythroplakia, dysphagia, or cervical lymphadenopathy.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Oral Cavity; revised PDF pp. 161–162

Question: Why are white or red patches in the mouth taken seriously?
Answer: Some white or red patches can be premalignant or malignant changes. They need examination and sometimes biopsy to confirm what they are.
Answer Context: Oral leukoplakia and erythroplakia are clinically important mucosal findings because they may represent premalignant or malignant change. Persistent mucosal lesions, especially in high-risk patients with smoking or alcohol exposure, require careful oral examination and histological assessment when indicated.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Oral Cavity > Symptoms and Signs; revised PDF pp. 161–162

Question: Why is lower lip cancer linked with sun exposure?
Answer: The lower lip receives more sunlight exposure and is a common site for sun-related lip cancer, particularly in older fair-skinned men.
Answer Context: Lip carcinoma is among the more curable head and neck cancers and is associated with sun exposure. The lower lip is most often affected. Risk factors include older age, male sex, and fairer skin, with treatment depending on the lesion and its stage.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Oral Cavity > Carcinoma of Lip; revised PDF p. 162

Question: What parts of the throat can get pharyngeal cancer?
Answer: Pharyngeal cancer can involve the nasopharynx, oropharynx, or hypopharynx. Each region has different symptoms, risk factors, and treatment approaches.
Answer Context: The pharynx is divided into nasopharynx, oropharynx, and hypopharynx. Carcinomas in these regions differ in anatomical site, risk factors, clinical presentation, investigation, and management. Assessment usually involves head and neck examination, endoscopy, biopsy, imaging, and multidisciplinary planning.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Pharynx; revised PDF p. 163
Figure Captions: Figure 62: Divisions of the pharynx.

Question: Why can a tumour behind the nose cause hearing loss or fluid behind the ear?
Answer: The post-nasal space is close to the Eustachian tube opening. A tumour there can block the tube and cause middle-ear fluid, hearing loss, or ear discomfort.
Answer Context: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma arises in the postnasal space and can present with cervical lymphadenopathy, ear pain, secretory otitis media, hearing loss, cranial nerve palsies, nasal obstruction, epistaxis, or postnasal drip. Eustachian tube obstruction explains middle-ear effusion and conductive hearing symptoms.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Pharynx > Carcinoma of the Nasopharynx; revised PDF p. 164

Question: Why is HPV mentioned when discussing tonsil or tongue-base cancer?
Answer: Some oropharyngeal cancers, especially those involving the tonsil or tongue base, are strongly associated with HPV types 16 and 18.
Answer Context: Oropharyngeal carcinoma includes tumours of the tongue base and tonsils. Risk factors include smoking, alcohol, and strong association with HPV 16 and HPV 18. HPV-related disease is increasingly seen in younger adults, including some non-smokers.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Pharynx > Carcinoma of the Oropharynx; revised PDF p. 165

Question: Why are swallowing difficulty, ear pain, and voice change concerning together?
Answer: This combination can occur with hypopharyngeal or other throat cancers, particularly when symptoms are progressive or associated with neck lumps.
Answer Context: Hypopharyngeal cancers often arise in the pyriform sinus and are usually squamous cell carcinomas. Symptoms may include cervical lymphadenopathy, pain radiating from throat to ear, sore throat, progressive dysphagia or odynophagia, and voice changes. Metastases are common at presentation.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Pharynx > Carcinoma of the Hypopharynx; revised PDF p. 166

Question: Why is a hoarse voice for several weeks treated as a warning sign?
Answer: Persistent or worsening hoarseness can be an early sign of vocal cord disease, including laryngeal cancer. It should be assessed, especially in smokers or heavy alcohol users.
Answer Context: Laryngeal carcinoma most commonly affects the glottis, where the true vocal folds are located. Hoarseness is the most common presentation overall and is especially common in glottic cancer, often worsening over 6–12 weeks. Smoking is the most important risk factor, with alcohol adding cumulative risk.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Larynx > Symptoms and Risk Factors; revised PDF pp. 167–168
Figure Captions: Figure 63: Left glottic squamous cell carcinoma- encroaching on the anterior commissure (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why would noisy breathing with throat symptoms be urgent?
Answer: Noisy breathing or stridor can mean the airway is narrowed. In laryngeal disease, this can be serious and needs urgent assessment.
Answer Context: Laryngeal carcinoma may present with hoarseness, cough, haemoptysis, painful swallowing, dysphagia, neck lymphadenopathy, and noisy breathing or stridor. Stridor is particularly concerning because it suggests airway compromise and may occur as a late presentation in supraglottic cancer.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Larynx > Symptoms; revised PDF p. 168
Figure Captions: Figure 63: Left glottic squamous cell carcinoma- encroaching on the anterior commissure (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why does treatment for voice-box cancer vary so much between patients?
Answer: Treatment depends on tumour size, location, spread, and staging. Small tumours may need one treatment, while larger tumours may require combined treatments or surgery.
Answer Context: Laryngeal carcinoma is staged using TNM classification and discussed by a multidisciplinary team. Smaller T1 or T2 tumours may be treated with single-modality radiotherapy or endoscopic laser excision. Larger T3 or T4 tumours may require chemoradiotherapy or laryngectomy with postoperative radiotherapy.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Carcinoma of the Larynx > Treatment; revised PDF p. 168
Figure Captions: Figure 63: Left glottic squamous cell carcinoma- encroaching on the anterior commissure (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Can reflux cause throat clearing and hoarseness even without heartburn?
Answer: Yes. Laryngopharyngeal reflux can cause throat symptoms such as hoarseness, throat clearing, chronic cough, globus, and swallowing discomfort even when heartburn is absent.
Answer Context: Laryngopharyngeal reflux describes upper respiratory tract symptoms caused by irritation from gastric contents. Common symptoms include hoarseness, throat clearing, chronic cough, globus pharyngeus, and dysphagia. Heartburn and indigestion correlate poorly with LPR, so absence of typical reflux symptoms does not exclude it.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Laryngopharyngeal Reflux; revised PDF p. 169

Question: Why was I advised not to eat close to bedtime for throat reflux?
Answer: Eating near bedtime can increase reflux exposure when lying down. Lifestyle measures, including avoiding late meals, can reduce throat irritation in some patients.
Answer Context: Conservative LPR management includes avoiding food within three hours of sleep, stopping smoking, reducing alcohol, addressing obesity, avoiding fizzy drinks, and reducing throat clearing. Speech therapy, alginates, and sometimes proton pump inhibitors may be used, though evidence for PPIs is limited.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Laryngopharyngeal Reflux > Treatment; revised PDF p. 170

Question: Is snoring the same as sleep apnoea?
Answer: No. Snoring is noisy breathing from upper airway resistance, but obstructive sleep apnoea involves repeated breathing pauses or reduced airflow with oxygen drops and sleep arousal.
Answer Context: Snoring reflects upper airway resistance and is common. Obstructive sleep apnoea involves apnoea lasting more than 10 seconds or hypopnoea with oxygen desaturation and arousal. Not all snorers have OSA, although most patients with OSA snore. Severity is classified using the apnoea-hypopnoea index.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Snoring and Obstructive Sleep Apnoea; revised PDF p. 171

Question: Why does the doctor ask my partner about my sleep?
Answer: A partner may notice breath-holding, gasping, choking, restlessness, or loud snoring that you may not remember. This helps screen for obstructive sleep apnoea.
Answer Context: OSA history should include witnessed breath-holding, gasping, choking, restlessness, daytime sleepiness, and irritability. Partner history is useful because the patient may not observe nocturnal events. Some families record sleep patterns to demonstrate apnoea-like episodes.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Snoring and Obstructive Sleep Apnoea > Symptoms; revised PDF p. 172

Question: Why are weight, neck size, nose blockage, tonsils, and jaw shape checked for sleep apnoea?
Answer: These factors can narrow or collapse the airway during sleep. The examination looks for the level or levels where airflow may be obstructed.
Answer Context: OSA assessment includes BMI, neck circumference, maxillofacial structure, retrognathia or prognathia, large tongue, large uvula, large tonsils, elongated palate, and nasal-to-laryngeal obstruction. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy helps assess the airway from nose to vocal cords.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Snoring and Obstructive Sleep Apnoea > Diagnosis; revised PDF p. 172

Question: What treatments are used for obstructive sleep apnoea?
Answer: Treatment may include weight loss, reducing alcohol, changing sleep position, nasal treatment, oral appliances, CPAP, or surgery in selected cases. The right option depends on severity and cause.
Answer Context: OSA management includes lifestyle modification, positional strategies, nasal dilators, mandibular advancement devices, CPAP to splint the airway, and treatment of nasal obstruction. Surgery targets anatomical obstruction and is common in children through adenotonsillectomy, while adult surgery requires careful selection and counselling.
Handbook Reference: Head and Neck > Snoring and Obstructive Sleep Apnoea > Treatment; revised PDF p. 173

Question: My child is making a harsh noisy sound when breathing in. Should I wait and see if it settles?
Answer: No. Noisy breathing on inspiration, especially stridor, can indicate upper airway obstruction and should be treated as urgent. The child should be assessed immediately, particularly if there is distress, blue colour change, agitation, or difficulty breathing.
Answer Context: Acute airway obstruction is a time-critical emergency because partial obstruction can deteriorate into complete airway compromise. Inspiratory stridor suggests narrowing around the supraglottic or glottic airway, while associated tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, agitation, cyanosis, or reduced breath sounds indicate worsening respiratory effort or fatigue. Children can decompensate quickly because their airway reserve is smaller.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Acute Airways Obstruction

Question: Why do doctors call for senior help so quickly when someone has stridor?
Answer: Stridor can mean the airway is narrowing. ENT, anaesthetic, emergency, and paediatric teams may be needed early because treatment sometimes has to secure the airway urgently.
Answer Context: Airway obstruction management prioritises early recognition, oxygenation, and senior multidisciplinary support. Investigations are secondary to immediate management, and unstable patients should not be delayed for imaging. Depending on the cause and response, treatment may require oxygen, heliox, nebulised adrenaline, steroids, intubation, a nasopharyngeal airway, cricothyroidotomy, or tracheostomy.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Acute Airways Obstruction > Treatment

Question: Why might doctors avoid upsetting a child who has suspected airway swelling?
Answer: Agitation can worsen breathing difficulty and, in some airway infections, may precipitate sudden loss of the airway. Clinicians try to keep the child calm while arranging urgent expert care.
Answer Context: In suspected airway obstruction, unnecessary distress can worsen airflow limitation by increasing respiratory demand and dynamic airway collapse. Some conditions, such as epiglottitis, can deteriorate if examination or investigation agitates the patient. Airway assessment must therefore balance diagnostic information against the risk of triggering complete obstruction.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Acute Airways Obstruction > Investigations

Question: If my nose is bleeding heavily, should I sit back or lean forward?
Answer: You should lean forward and pinch the soft part of the nose firmly, not the hard bridge. Leaning forward reduces swallowing of blood and pressure over the soft septal area helps control common anterior bleeding.
Answer Context: Most nosebleeds arise from Little’s area on the anterior septum. First-line control uses direct compression of the soft cartilaginous part of the nose against the septum, with the head tilted forward. Pinching the bony bridge is ineffective because it does not compress the usual anterior bleeding site.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Epistaxis > Initial management
Figure Captions: Figure 64: Little areas on the left nasal cavity (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why did the doctor ask whether the blood was coming out the front of my nose or going down my throat?
Answer: That helps estimate whether the bleeding is more likely from the front or back of the nose. Posterior bleeding can be harder to control and may need packing, admission, or surgery.
Answer Context: Epistaxis assessment distinguishes anterior from posterior bleeding because this changes management. Anterior bleeding often presents from the nostril and may be visible around Little’s area. Posterior bleeding may trickle into the throat, be swallowed, and require posterior packing, admission, operative arterial ligation, or interventional radiology if conservative measures fail.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Epistaxis > History and management
Figure Captions: Figure 64: Little areas on the left nasal cavity (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why does the doctor ask about blood thinners, high blood pressure, liver problems, and easy bruising when I have a nosebleed?
Answer: These factors can make bleeding heavier, longer-lasting, or harder to control. They also help decide whether blood tests, admission, medication review, or more urgent treatment is needed.
Answer Context: Epistaxis severity is influenced by local triggers and systemic bleeding risk. Anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, hypertension, liver or kidney disease, alcohol-related disease, coagulopathy, pregnancy, hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia, and previous nasal surgery may alter bleeding tendency, resuscitation priorities, and procedural planning.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Epistaxis > Risk factors and causes
Figure Captions: Figure 64: Little areas on the left nasal cavity (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: My child has a bad-smelling discharge from only one nostril. Could something be stuck inside?
Answer: Yes. A one-sided, offensive nasal discharge in a child can suggest a nasal foreign body. It should be assessed because some objects can move backwards or damage the nose.
Answer Context: Nasal foreign bodies are common in children and may include beads, peas, nuts, sweets, or small household objects. Unilateral obstruction, offensive discharge, and irritability in infants are typical clues. The main risks are aspiration into the airway and local mucosal injury, especially if removal attempts push the object further posteriorly.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Foreign body in the nose
Figure Captions: Figure 65: Foreign body (screw) in the nose of a child.

Question: Why is a button battery in the nose treated as an emergency?
Answer: A button battery can quickly burn and erode the nasal lining and cartilage. It needs urgent removal to prevent septal damage and perforation.
Answer Context: Button batteries are chemically hazardous foreign bodies. When lodged in the nose, they can rapidly erode nasal mucosa and septal cartilage, causing necrosis and septal perforation. Management therefore prioritises prompt recognition and removal rather than routine observation.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Foreign body in the nose > Button battery risk
Figure Captions: Figure 65: Foreign body (screw) in the nose of a child.

Question: After a nose injury, why does the doctor urgently check the middle wall inside my nose?
Answer: They are checking for a septal haematoma, which is a collection of blood between the septal cartilage and its covering. If missed, it can damage the cartilage and permanently change the shape of the nose.
Answer Context: A septal haematoma forms when blood collects between the perichondrium and septal cartilage. This separates cartilage from its blood supply, causing devascularisation, necrosis, abscess formation, perforation, and saddle-nose deformity if untreated. It is commonly associated with nasal trauma and may be unilateral or bilateral.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Nasal septum haematoma > Description and complications
Figure Captions: Figure 66: Right sided septal haematoma. Description A septal haematoma is blood which collects between the perichondrium and septal cartilage. There is an increased risk of devascularisation of the septal cartilage and this can lead to necrosis, perforation and deformity.

Question: Why does a septal haematoma need surgery instead of just tablets or observation?
Answer: The trapped blood must be drained urgently, usually under anaesthetic. Antibiotics may be used, but drainage is needed to prevent cartilage death, abscess, perforation, and permanent deformity.
Answer Context: Septal haematoma management requires urgent incision and drainage because the mechanical separation between cartilage and perichondrium threatens cartilage viability. Delayed treatment increases the risk of septal abscess, perforation, and saddle-nose deformity. Antibiotic cover is used to reduce secondary infection risk, but it does not replace drainage.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Nasal septum haematoma > Management
Figure Captions: Figure 66: Right sided septal haematoma. Description A septal haematoma is blood which collects between the perichondrium and septal cartilage. There is an increased risk of devascularisation of the septal cartilage and this can lead to necrosis, perforation and deformity.

Question: My child put something in their ear but seems fine. Does it still need to come out?
Answer: Yes. Ear foreign bodies should be removed with appropriate equipment. Even if symptoms are mild, retained objects can cause hearing loss, discharge, bleeding, infection, or canal injury.
Answer Context: Ear foreign bodies are common in children but may also occur in adults, including cotton-bud fragments or tissue. Symptoms can include hearing loss and foul-smelling or bloody discharge if the object has been present for some time. Removal technique depends on the object’s shape, material, and position.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Foreign body in the ear
Figure Captions: Figure 67: Foreign body in the ear (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why should I not keep trying to pull an object out of my child’s ear at home?
Answer: Repeated attempts can push the object deeper or injure the ear canal or eardrum. A clinician can choose the correct tool, such as forceps, a hook, suction, or microscopy.
Answer Context: Foreign-body removal requires appropriate visualisation and instrumentation. Hard round objects may need a hook, soft objects may be grasped with forceps, and some cases need microsuction or removal under general anaesthesia. The first attempt is often the best chance, especially in children, so poorly controlled attempts increase trauma and failure risk.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Foreign body in the ear; Practical Procedures > Oto-Microscopy and Foreign Body Removal
Figure Captions: Figure 67: Foreign body in the ear (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: I felt sudden ear pain after a blow to the ear and then noticed blood. Could my eardrum be torn?
Answer: Yes, trauma can perforate the eardrum and may cause sudden pain, bleeding, hearing loss, or later discharge. You should have the ear examined and keep water out until reviewed.
Answer Context: Tympanic membrane perforation may follow trauma, barotrauma, infection, or previous ear surgery such as grommet insertion. Pain may occur at the moment of perforation, sometimes with blood discharge. Hearing loss and recurrent discharge can occur depending on perforation size, infection, and middle-ear involvement.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Perforated Tympanic Membrane > Causes and presentation
Figure Captions: Figure 68: Central perforation of left tympanic membrane (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Question: Why was I told to keep water out of my ear after an eardrum perforation?
Answer: Water can enter through the perforation and irritate or infect the middle ear. Keeping the ear dry helps healing and reduces the risk of recurrent discharge.
Answer Context: Conservative management of many tympanic membrane perforations includes water precautions. Traumatic or infection-related perforations often heal within 6–8 weeks, but water entry through the perforation can introduce infection and prolong otorrhoea. Persistent perforation, recurrent infection, or desire to waterproof the ear may lead to myringoplasty or tympanoplasty consideration.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Perforated Tympanic Membrane > Management
Figure Captions: Figure 68: Central perforation of left tympanic membrane (reproduced with permission from Otolaryngology Houston, www.ghoryeb.com).

Subsection: FB in the Pharynx or Oesophagus
Question: My child may have swallowed a coin. Why are doctors so worried it could be a battery?
Answer: Button batteries can look like coins on X-ray but are much more dangerous. They can cause chemical burns and perforate the oesophagus within hours, so they need urgent removal.
Answer Context: In children, pharyngeal or oesophageal foreign bodies are often inanimate objects such as coins, but button batteries must always be considered. A lodged battery can rapidly cause caustic injury, tissue necrosis, and oesophageal perforation, making it a surgical emergency comparable in urgency to major bleeding or airway compromise.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > FB in the pharynx or oesophagus > Battery ingestion

Subsection: FB in the Pharynx or Oesophagus
Question: I feel food stuck in my throat. Why does the doctor ask whether there was a bone in it?
Answer: A bone makes the situation more urgent because sharp foreign bodies can perforate the throat or oesophagus. Food without bone may sometimes pass, but bone-containing boluses need prompt removal.
Answer Context: Adult oesophageal foreign-body presentations often involve food bolus obstruction. The presence of bone is a key risk factor because sharp or rigid material increases the chance of perforation. Symptoms such as dysphagia, odynophagia, drooling, immediate regurgitation, or delayed regurgitation help estimate obstruction level and urgency.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > FB in the pharynx or oesophagus > Adult food bolus

Question: Why is a dental infection in the jaw or floor of the mouth treated so seriously?
Answer: Some dental infections can spread into deep neck spaces and threaten the airway. Symptoms such as swelling, difficulty opening the mouth, swallowing trouble, drooling, voice change, or noisy breathing need urgent assessment.
Answer Context: Deep neck space infections are usually bacterial and may originate from poor dental hygiene, particularly in Ludwig’s angina. Because infection can spread through fascial compartments, patients may develop pain, trismus, dysphagia, dysphonia, drooling, stridor, fever, and malaise. Airway compromise is a central danger.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Deep neck space infections > Causes and presentation
Figure Captions: Figure 69: Ludwig’s Angina (courtesy of E-lfENT)

Question: Why would someone with a deep neck infection need a CT scan and surgery?
Answer: CT with contrast helps show which neck spaces are infected. If there is an abscess, drainage may be needed, along with fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and airway support if breathing is threatened.
Answer Context: Management of deep neck infection begins with an ABC approach because patients may be systemically unwell and the airway may be at risk. Contrast CT helps confirm the diagnosis and map involved compartments. Definitive treatment often requires surgical drainage, with microbiology from pus or blood cultures guiding targeted antibiotic therapy.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Deep neck space infections > Management
Figure Captions: Figure 69: Ludwig’s Angina (courtesy of E-lfENT) || Figure 70: Right quinsy (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: What is quinsy, and why does it make the voice sound strange?
Answer: Quinsy is a peritonsillar abscess. It can cause severe throat pain, painful swallowing, difficulty opening the mouth, and a muffled “hot potato” voice.
Answer Context: Quinsy is a peritonsillar abscess, where pus collects around the tonsil. The swelling distorts the oropharyngeal space and can produce odynophagia, trismus, and a muffled voice. Treatment is usually per-oral aspiration or incision and drainage, often possible under local anaesthetic.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Deep neck space infections > Quinsy
Figure Captions: Figure 70: Right quinsy (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Someone has a knife wound to the neck but is awake and talking. Why is it still an emergency?
Answer: The neck contains major blood vessels, the airway, the food pipe, nerves, and the spine. A patient can initially look stable but still have serious hidden injury, so urgent trauma assessment is required.
Answer Context: Penetrating neck trauma may result from knives, broken glass, or gunshot wounds. The injury site helps predict structures at risk, but clinical appearance alone may not exclude vascular, airway, tracheal, oesophageal, cervical spine, skull base, or cranial nerve injury. Initial management follows ATLS priorities.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Penetrating neck trauma > Causes and presentation
Figure Captions: Figure 71: Knife laceration to neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT). || Figure 72: Zones of the Neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT). || Figure 73: Important landmarks in the neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why does management of a neck stab wound start with airway, breathing, and circulation rather than just stitching the cut?
Answer: The immediate priority is to identify and control life-threatening airway or bleeding problems. The visible wound may be less important than injuries to deeper neck structures.
Answer Context: Penetrating neck trauma is managed using the ATLS sequence: airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. The airway may need intubation or surgical access. In stable patients, imaging such as CT angiography and CT head and neck may assess vascular, intracranial, cervical spine, tracheal, or oesophageal injury before deciding on exploration or observation.
Handbook Reference: ENT Emergencies > Penetrating neck trauma > Management
Figure Captions: Figure 71: Knife laceration to neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT). || Figure 72: Zones of the Neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT). || Figure 73: Important landmarks in the neck (courtesy of Elf-ENT).

Question: Why did the doctor avoid amoxicillin when they mentioned possible glandular fever?
Answer: Amoxicillin is usually avoided when glandular fever is possible because it can trigger a widespread skin reaction. The clinician may choose another approach depending on the suspected cause of the sore throat.
Answer Context: Sore throat treatment requires distinction between likely bacterial tonsillitis and viral illnesses such as glandular fever. Phenoxymethylpenicillin may be used for bacterial sore throat, whereas amoxicillin is avoided when glandular fever is possible because of the risk of systemic rash and severe skin reaction. Antibiotic choice therefore reflects both diagnosis and medication safety.
Handbook Reference: Medications in ENT > Antibiotics > Phenoxymethylpenicillin and Amoxicillin

Question: Why was I told that some ear drops may not be suitable if I have a hole in my eardrum or grommets?
Answer: Some ear drops, especially aminoglycoside-containing drops, are used cautiously when the eardrum is perforated or grommets are present because of possible inner-ear toxicity. Safer alternatives may be considered by the clinician.
Answer Context: Topical ear antibiotics can deliver high local treatment concentration for external or middle-ear discharge, but tympanic membrane integrity matters. Aminoglycoside drops may be useful for otitis externa, including pseudomonal infection, yet require caution with perforation or grommets because of ototoxicity risk. Non-ototoxic quinolone drops are often preferred in ears with perforation or chronic suppurative otitis media.
Handbook Reference: Medications in ENT > Antibiotics > Aminoglycosides and Quinolones

Question: Why does my nasal steroid spray not work immediately?
Answer: Nasal steroid sprays usually need regular use for a few weeks before their full effect is noticed. They reduce inflammation gradually, especially in allergic rhinitis and chronic rhinosinusitis.
Answer Context: Topical nasal steroids act on inflamed nasal and sinonasal mucosa rather than giving instant decongestion. They are commonly used for allergic rhinitis and chronic rhinosinusitis with or without polyps. A two-to-three-week lead-in period is expected, and lack of improvement after consistent use for about eight weeks may prompt reassessment or alternative therapy.
Handbook Reference: Medications in ENT > Steroids > Topical nasal steroids

Question: Why are steroid tablets or steroid injections used for some ENT problems but not casually?
Answer: Steroids can be useful in selected ENT conditions, such as significant nasal polyps, Bell’s palsy, or sudden sensorineural hearing loss, but they must be used carefully because systemic steroids can have important side effects.
Answer Context: Steroids are used selectively when inflammation, nerve recovery, or inner-ear pathology is clinically relevant. Oral steroids may be used with topical nasal steroids for significant nasal polyposis, prescribed early in Bell’s palsy to improve facial nerve recovery, and considered in sudden sensorineural hearing loss. Intratympanic dexamethasone can deliver steroid locally to the ear while limiting systemic exposure.
Handbook Reference: Medications in ENT > Steroids > Oral steroids and intratympanic steroids

Question: Why do antihistamines help my sneezing and runny nose more than my blocked nose?
Answer: Antihistamines are particularly useful for sneezing, watery discharge, and itchy eyes. Nasal obstruction is often better treated with intranasal steroids because blockage is more related to mucosal swelling.
Answer Context: Antihistamines counter histamine-mediated allergic symptoms and are useful when rhinitis is dominated by sneezing, rhinorrhoea, and eye itching. They may be used alone or alongside topical corticosteroids. Systemic antihistamines can cause sedation, dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, and acute angle glaucoma, so symptom pattern and adverse-effect risk guide selection.
Handbook Reference: Medications in ENT > Antihistamines

Question: Why was I told not to keep taking vertigo tablets for too long?
Answer: Some vestibular sedatives can help nausea and vertigo in the short term, but prolonged use may slow the brain’s natural compensation after an inner-ear balance problem. They are usually not intended for long-term unsupervised use.
Answer Context: Vestibular sedatives reduce nausea and vertigo symptoms but can interfere with central compensation, the adaptive process by which the brain recalibrates after vestibular disturbance. Prochlorperazine is commonly used for nausea and vertigo in labyrinthitis but should not usually continue beyond about two weeks. Cinnarizine is another option for nausea and vertigo. Meniere’s disease treatments may include betahistine or intratympanic dexamethasone or gentamicin, with gentamicin requiring caution because it is ototoxic.
Handbook Reference: Medications in ENT > Vestibular sedatives & Meniere’s disease

Question: Why would the doctor burn a tiny blood vessel inside my nose?
Answer: The doctor may use silver nitrate cautery to seal a visible bleeding point, usually on the front part of the nasal septum. This is commonly done when the source of recurrent or active anterior nosebleeds can be clearly seen.
Answer Context: Anterior epistaxis commonly arises from prominent vessels over Little’s area on the anterior septum. Chemical cautery works by applying silver nitrate directly to the bleeding vessel after the nose has been decongested and anaesthetised. The vasoconstrictive effect reduces active bleeding, while cautery seals the local vascular point.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Nasal Cautery
Figure Captions: Figure 74: Prominent vessels over Little’s area on the right. || Figure 75: Silver nitrate sticks. || Figure 76: Cautery Applied in a ‘Rose Petal’ Pattern to to fully deal with the efferent and afferent tributary vessels.

Question: Why does the doctor spray or put medicine-soaked cotton in my nose before cautery?
Answer: The spray or pledget usually numbs and decongests the nose. This improves comfort, reduces bleeding, and makes the bleeding point easier to see.
Answer Context: Local anaesthetic and vasoconstrictor preparation improves procedural tolerance and visualisation. Lignocaine reduces discomfort, while phenylephrine constricts nasal mucosal vessels. Better control of septal oozing allows more precise cautery of the visible anterior bleeding point rather than broader or repeated chemical application.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Nasal Cautery > Preparation
Figure Captions: Figure 75: Silver nitrate sticks.

Question: Why should both sides of the nasal septum not be cauterised directly opposite each other?
Answer: Cauterising matching areas on both sides can reduce the blood supply to the septal cartilage and increase the risk of a hole forming in the septum.
Answer Context: Septal cartilage depends on its overlying mucoperichondrial blood supply. Chemical injury on directly opposing sides of the septum can produce local ischaemia, impaired healing, and septal perforation. Cautery is therefore applied carefully, often in a controlled “rose-petal” pattern around the vessel rather than indiscriminately across both septal surfaces.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Nasal Cautery > Technique and cautions
Figure Captions: Figure 76: Cautery Applied in a ‘Rose Petal’ Pattern to to fully deal with the efferent and afferent tributary vessels.

Question: Why would my nose need packing if cautery was already tried?
Answer: Packing may be used when the bleeding point cannot be found or when cautery does not control the bleeding. The pack applies pressure and helps stabilise clot formation inside the nose.
Answer Context: Anterior nasal packing is used when an anterior bleeding source is not visible, cannot be safely cauterised, or remains uncontrolled after cautery. Packs such as Merocel or Rapid Rhino support clot formation and apply pressure between the septum and inferior turbinate, thereby tamponading bleeding vessels.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Anterior Nasal Packing
Figure Captions: Figure 77: Nasal Packing with Rapid Rhino nasal pack. It should be inserted along the floor of the hard-palate (direction of arrow) and NOT upwards.

Question: Why does the nurse or doctor insert the nasal pack straight back rather than upwards?
Answer: The pack should follow the floor of the nose, not point upward toward the skull base. This follows the normal nasal passage and reduces the risk of trauma.
Answer Context: Safe nasal pack insertion follows the nasal floor along the hard palate, aiming between the septum and inferior turbinate. Upward insertion is anatomically incorrect and may injure nasal structures. Septal deviation or anatomical variation can make insertion difficult, so forceful placement should be avoided.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Anterior Nasal Packing > Technique
Figure Captions: Figure 77: Nasal Packing with Rapid Rhino nasal pack. It should be inserted along the floor of the hard-palate (direction of arrow) and NOT upwards.

Question: Why do I need pain relief and observation after a nasal pack is inserted?
Answer: Nasal packing can be uncomfortable and bleeding can continue behind the pack. Pain relief, reassessment, and sometimes admission are used to ensure the bleeding is controlled and that you remain stable.
Answer Context: Nasal packs can cause significant pressure discomfort and require adequate analgesia. After insertion, the patient should be reassessed to confirm bleeding control, including checking the posterior pharyngeal wall for ongoing posterior bleeding. Continued resuscitation, correction of clotting problems, and hospital admission may be required depending on severity and comorbidity.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Anterior Nasal Packing > Aftercare and reassessment
Figure Captions: Figure 77: Nasal Packing with Rapid Rhino nasal pack. It should be inserted along the floor of the hard-palate (direction of arrow) and NOT upwards.

Question: Why would an ENT doctor pass a small camera through my nose?
Answer: Nasendoscopy allows the clinician to inspect areas that cannot be seen well with a simple torch or speculum, including the nasal cavity, post-nasal space, throat, voice box, and upper swallowing area.
Answer Context: Flexible nasendoscopy uses a fibreoptic endoscope to examine the nose, postnasal space, pharynx, and larynx. It is useful for assessing nasal and sinus pathology, voice symptoms, swallowing problems, airway concerns, and anatomical areas such as the Eustachian tube orifice, fossa of Rosenmüller, tongue base, epiglottis, pyriform fossae, and vocal folds.
Handbook Reference: Practical Procedures in ENT > Nasendoscopy > Description and indications
Figure Captions: Figure 78: Flexible nasendoscopy. || Figure 79: Flexible nasendoscopy schematic.

Question: Will I always need numbing spray before a nose camera test?
Answer: Not always. Many patients tolerate flexible nasendoscopy without anaesthetic spray, but local anaesthetic and decongestant spray may be used if needed.
Answer Context: Flexible nasendoscopy is commonly performed in the outpatient setting and is often tolerated without topical anaesthesia. When required, local anaesthetic and decongestant spray can improve comfort and nasal patency. The patient is usually seated upright with slight head flexion while the lubricated endoscope is gently advanced.
